elements.sent-2411-009: The field of AI-assisted music creation has made significant strides, yet existing systems often struggle to meet the demands of iterative and nuanced music production.
elements.sent-2411-010: These challenges include providing sufficient control over the generated content and allowing for flexible, precise edits.
elements.sent-2411-011: This thesis tackles these issues by introducing a series of advancements that progressively build upon each other, enhancing the controllability and editability of text-to-music generation models.
elements.sent-2411-012: First, I introduce Loop Copilot, a system that tries to address the need for iterative refinement in music creation.
elements.sent-2411-013: Loop Copilot leverages a large language model (LLM) to coordinate multiple specialised AI models, enabling users to generate and refine music interactively through a conversational interface.
elements.sent-2411-014: Central to this system is the Global Attribute Table, which records and maintains key musical attributes throughout the iterative process, ensuring that modifications at any stage preserve the overall coherence of the music.
elements.sent-2411-015: While Loop Copilot excels in orchestrating the music creation process, it does not directly address the need for detailed edits to the generated content.
elements.sent-2411-016: To overcome this limitation, MusicMagus is presented as a further solution for editing AI-generated music.
elements.sent-2411-017: MusicMagus introduces a zero-shot text-to-music editing approach that allows for the modification of specific musical attributes, such as genre, mood, and instrumentation, without the need for retraining.
elements.sent-2411-018: By manipulating the latent space within pre-trained diffusion models, MusicMagus ensures that these edits are stylistically coherent and that non-targeted attributes remain unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-019: This system is particularly effective in maintaining the structural integrity of the music during edits, but it encounters challenges with more complex and real-world audio scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-020: Building on the advancements of the previous systems, Instruct-MusicGen tries to address the remaining limitations by incorporating instruction tuning into the MusicGen model.
elements.sent-2411-021: This approach enables precise and efficient editing of music through text-based instructions, such as adding, removing, or modifying specific musical stems.
elements.sent-2411-022: Instruct-MusicGen integrates a text fusion module and an audio fusion module, allowing the model to process instructions and audio inputs concurrently and produce high-quality edited music.
elements.sent-2411-023: This system not only achieves greater precision in edits but also broadens the applicability of music language models to more complex and dynamic production environments, offering a scalable and efficient solution.
elements.sent-2411-024: Collectively, these contributions form a robust framework that significantly enhances the controllability and editability of AI systems in music production.
elements.sent-2411-025: By progressively addressing the limitations of each previous approach, this thesis advances the state of the art in AI-assisted music creation, enabling more flexible, precise, and dynamic music production processes.

elements.sent-2411-067: The convergence of artificial intelligence (AI) and music creation is reshaping the landscape of artistic expression and production workflows.
elements.sent-2411-068: Over the past decade, advances in AI-generated content (AIGC), driven by techniques like deep learning and large language models (LLMs), have enabled AI systems to compose, arrange, and even improvise music in ways that come close to human creativity.
elements.sent-2411-069: These systems can now generate full-length compositions~\citep{sd2 , assist in songwriting~\citep{songcomposer, singsong , and offer personalised recommendations for sound design~\citep{iteratta , marking a shift in how music is created and consumed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Recent breakthroughs, such as Stable Audio~\citep{sd1, sd2 , MusicLM~\citep{musiclm , and MusicGen~\citep{musicgen , have demonstrated the power of large-scale music foundation models to generate high-quality music pieces.}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: While these systems have made significant strides in enabling users with minimal formal musical training to generate complete music compositions, they are still in the early stages of being adopted by professionals.
elements.sent-2411-072: Several critical challenges remain, particularly when it comes to meeting the nuanced and iterative requirements of professional music production.
elements.sent-2411-073: While AI-generated music tools have made music creation more accessible and efficient, they fall short in key areas:.
elements.sent-2411-074: Controllability: Current AI music systems can generate high-quality music but often lack the fine-grained control required by musicians and producers.
elements.sent-2411-075: Professional music production demands precision, where users must control and modify specific musical elements such as rhythm, harmony, instrumentation, and emotional tone.
elements.sent-2411-076: However, AI systems frequently produce music that is difficult to adjust on a detailed level, making it hard to tailor the output to the artist's vision.
elements.sent-2411-077: Editability: Music production is an iterative process.
elements.sent-2411-078: Artists seldom create a final product in a single step; they refine and rework their compositions over multiple iterations.
elements.sent-2411-079: However, most AI tools treat music generation as a one-off process, where users have little or no capacity to modify the generated content without starting from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-080: This lack of editability limits the practicality of these tools in real-world production environments, where iterative refinements are the norm.
elements.sent-2411-081: Intuitive Interaction: Although AI tools have advanced considerably, many still require users to engage with complex interfaces or possess technical knowledge, such as understanding model parameters or programming languages.
elements.sent-2411-082: For musicians focused on creativity, such technical barriers can be a major deterrent, preventing widespread adoption of AI technologies in music production.
elements.sent-2411-083: An intuitive, natural interface that allows musicians to interact with AI systems conversationally—rather than through technical inputs—could unlock the true potential of these tools.
elements.sent-2411-084: These limitations underscore a gap between the current capabilities of AI music systems and the demands of musicians.
elements.sent-2411-085: As the boundaries of AI-generated content continue to expand, addressing these shortcomings is essential not only for increasing the accessibility of AI tools but also for ensuring that AI systems truly augment human creativity without compromising artistic control.
elements.sent-2411-086: In light of these challenges, the motivation for this thesis is to explore how AI systems can be enhanced to offer more control, editability, and user-friendly interactions in music creation.
elements.sent-2411-087: This research seeks to bridge the gap between current AI capabilities and the needs of professional and amateur musicians by proposing a series of solutions that push AI music systems closer to real-world applicability, ultimately empowering artists to harness AI as a collaborative creative tool.
elements.sent-2411-088: The primary aim of this thesis is to develop AI-assisted music systems that overcome the limitations identified in controllability, editability, and user interaction.
elements.sent-2411-089: These systems are intended to bridge the gap between the existing capabilities of AI-generated content tools and the practical needs of music production.
elements.sent-2411-090: The overarching objective is to design systems that allow musicians and producers to interact with AI in a more intuitive and seamless manner, while retaining control over the fine-grained details of their compositions.
elements.sent-2411-091: This research aims to extend the creative possibilities of AI, not just by generating new music, but by empowering users to refine, edit, and direct the output in ways that align with their artistic intent.
elements.sent-2411-092: Specifically, this thesis investigates how AI systems can be structured to support iterative editing and natural language control within the music creation process, paving the way for more versatile and collaborative AI music tools.
elements.sent-2411-093: To achieve this aim, the following key research questions will be addressed:.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Research Question 1: How can we design \highlight{text-to-music models}
elements.sent-2411-094: This research question explores how models can be designed to allow users to control and adjust these musical elements in a way that integrates naturally into their creative workflows.
elements.sent-2411-095: By embedding controllability into AI music models, the goal is to provide users with direct manipulation of individual components, facilitating the creation of music that matches their vision.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Research Question 2: How can AI systems support iterative and flexible editing of AI-generated music, allowing musicians to refine their compositions seamlessly?
elements.sent-2411-096: Human music creation is inherently iterative, requiring continuous adjustments and refinements.
elements.sent-2411-097: This question examines how AI models can support iterative workflows, where musicians can easily modify generated content without starting from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-098: By enabling flexible editing, the research seeks to develop systems that preserve the structural integrity of the music while allowing specific attributes to be reworked in response to user feedback.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Research Question 3: How can AI music systems be made more intuitive, allowing users to interact with these technologies in a more natural manner?
elements.sent-2411-099: The technical complexity of current AI music tools often creates a barrier to their adoption.
elements.sent-2411-100: This question focuses on designing systems that allow users to interact with AI music tools in more natural and intuitive ways.
elements.sent-2411-101: Possible directions include the use of conversational interfaces or other simplified control mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-102: The goal is to lower the technical barrier for musicians, enabling more synergistic collaboration between human creativity and AI-generated content.
elements.sent-2411-103: The thesis is organised as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-104: Introduction This chapter provides the motivation behind the research, outlines the aims and objectives of the thesis, and presents an overview of the structure and contributions.
elements.sent-2411-105: It sets the stage for the subsequent chapters by highlighting the challenges in current AI-assisted music creation systems and introducing the proposed solutions.
elements.sent-2411-106: Background This chapter offers a comprehensive review of the existing literature and technologies in AI-assisted music creation.
elements.sent-2411-107: It covers fundamental concepts in music generation, text-to-music synthesis, and controllable music generation.
elements.sent-2411-108: The chapter also introduces key technologies underlying the work, such as large language models, diffusion models, and music language models.
elements.sent-2411-109: It concludes by identifying gaps in current research, providing context for the contributions.
elements.sent-2411-110: Loop Copilot: Conducting AI Ensembles for Music Generation and Iterative Editing This chapter introduces Loop Copilot, the first major contribution.
elements.sent-2411-111: It details the system's architecture, including the integration of multiple specialised AI models coordinated by a large language model.
elements.sent-2411-112: The chapter explains the concept of the Global Attribute Table and its role in maintaining musical coherence during iterative editing.
elements.sent-2411-113: It also presents a user study evaluating the system's effectiveness and limitations in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-114: MusicMagus: Zero-Shot Text-to-Music Editing via Diffusion Models This chapter presents MusicMagus, the second major contribution.
elements.sent-2411-115: It explores the use of diffusion models for zero-shot text-guided music editing.
elements.sent-2411-116: The chapter details the methodology for manipulating the latent space to achieve specific musical attribute modifications while preserving overall structure.
elements.sent-2411-117: It discusses the system's strengths in music editing and its limitations with more complex operations.
elements.sent-2411-118: Instruct-MusicGen: Unlocking Text-to-Music Editing for Music Language Models via Instruction Tuning This chapter introduces Instruct-MusicGen, the most advanced contribution.
elements.sent-2411-119: It describes the integration of instruction tuning with music language models to enable precise, text-guided editing of musical content.
elements.sent-2411-120: The chapter describes how the system combines both text and audio inputs to carry out complex edits.
elements.sent-2411-121: It also discusses the system's capabilities in handling a wide range of editing tasks and its potential impact on professional music production workflows.
elements.sent-2411-122: Conclusions and Future Work The final chapter summarises the key findings and contributions of the thesis.
elements.sent-2411-123: It reflects on how the presented work advances the field of AI-assisted music creation and discusses its potential impact on the music industry.
elements.sent-2411-124: The chapter also identifies remaining challenges and outlines promising directions for future research, including potential improvements to the proposed systems and new avenues for exploration in AI-music interaction.
elements.sent-2411-125: The contributions of this thesis represent significant advancements in the field of AI-assisted music creation, particularly in enhancing the controllability and editability of text-to-music generation systems.
elements.sent-2411-126: This thesis introduces novel approaches that progressively address the limitations of existing systems, culminating in a framework for flexible and precise music editing.
elements.sent-2411-127: The main contributions of this thesis are:.
elements.sent-2411-128: Loop Copilot: An LLM-Orchestrated Multi-Model System for Iterative Music Creation \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Developed a system that leverages a large language model to coordinate multiple specialised AI music models.
elements.sent-2411-130: Introduced the Global Attribute Table (GAT) concept for maintaining musical coherence during iterative editing.
elements.sent-2411-131: Demonstrated the feasibility of using natural language interactions for complex music generation and editing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-132: Conducted a user study to evaluate the system's effectiveness in real-world music production scenarios.. \end{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-133: MusicMagus: Zero-Shot Text-to-Music Editing via Diffusion Models \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Pioneered a novel approach for zero-shot text-guided music editing using pre-trained diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-135: Developed techniques for manipulating the latent space of diffusion models to achieve specific musical attribute modifications.
elements.sent-2411-136: Demonstrated the ability to perform stylistically coherent edits without the need for additional training.
elements.sent-2411-137: Addressed limitations in stem level editing and preserving non-targeted musical elements during edits.. \end{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Instruct-MusicGen: Text-to-Music Editing for Music Language Models via Instruction Tuning \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Applied instruction tuning to the MusicGen model to enable precise and efficient editing through text-based instructions.
elements.sent-2411-140: Developed a dual-modality fusion process for concurrent processing of text instructions and audio inputs.
elements.sent-2411-141: Expanded the capabilities of music language models to handle complex editing tasks, including adding, removing, or modifying specific musical stems.
elements.sent-2411-142: Demonstrated the scalability and efficiency of the system in handling a wide range of music production scenarios.. \end{itemize.}

elements.sent-2411-176: In this chapter, I explore the fundamental concepts and state-of-the-art methods central to this thesis, focusing on text-to-music backbone models and techniques for enhancing their controllability and editability.
elements.sent-2411-177: I begin by examining the basic knowledge underlying music and text representations, including symbolic, audio, and time-frequency representations, as well as modern music encoders and cross-modal representations.
elements.sent-2411-178: I then delve into two main categories of text-to-music backbone models: diffusion models and audio language models.
elements.sent-2411-179: For diffusion models, I discuss Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs), Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs), and related innovations.
elements.sent-2411-180: In the realm of audio language modelling, I explore Vector Quantised Variational Autoencoders (VQ-VAEs), Residual Vector Quantisation (RVQ), and autoregressive models, with a particular focus on MusicGen and its architecture, training, and performance.
elements.sent-2411-181: A comparison between diffusion models and audio language models is presented, highlighting the strengths and applications of each approach.
elements.sent-2411-182: I also introduce Parameter-Efficient Finetuning (PEFT) techniques, such as Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and Llama-Adapter, which play a crucial role in adapting pretrained models for specific tasks.
elements.sent-2411-183: Finally, I address the aspect of enriching controllability and editability for music generation models, which is critical to this thesis.
elements.sent-2411-184: This includes various approaches to enhancing control, specific techniques used in music generative models, and post-training generation models with control mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-185: I also explore specialised editing models and agent-based methods for compositional music generation and editing.
elements.sent-2411-186: This comprehensive background provides the foundation for understanding the advancements and innovations presented in subsequent chapters, setting the stage for our exploration of novel methodologies in text-to-music generation and control.
elements.sent-2411-187: Imagine I have a music audio track and its corresponding text description.
elements.sent-2411-188: Given a text, $x$: ``A romantic pop song with piano and guitar accompaniment." The corresponding music is represented in waveform as $y$.
elements.sent-2411-189: Before diving into formal definitions, I will use this example to illustrate related terms and concepts.
elements.sent-2411-190: The processing of text and music audio presents unique challenges due to their inherently unstructured nature.
elements.sent-2411-191: To enable effective analysis and generation, it is usually necessary to transform both text and music into structured data representations, often in the form of high-dimensional vectors or matrices (some models are capable of operating directly on raw audio data).
elements.sent-2411-192: These representations serve as the foundation for various downstream tasks, including natural language processing, music information retrieval, and cross-modal learning.
elements.sent-2411-193: At the most fundamental level, text data is stored as string objects within computational systems.
elements.sent-2411-194: However, the low-level nature of strings makes them unsuitable for the numerical operations required by machine learning algorithms, so they must first be transformed into more abstract, higher-level representations.
elements.sent-2411-195: Consequently, a series of transformations must be applied to convert raw text into a format amenable to computational analysis.
elements.sent-2411-196: The initial step in this process is tokenisation, a linguistic technique that decomposes a continuous string of text into discrete units called tokens.
elements.sent-2411-197: These tokens may represent individual words, subwords, or characters, depending on the chosen tokenisation strategy.
elements.sent-2411-198: Subsequently, each token is mapped to a numerical index based on a predefined vocabulary, effectively translating the text into a sequence of integers.
elements.sent-2411-199: Given the high-dimensional and sparse nature of natural language, it is advantageous to further transform these token sequences into dense vector representations.
elements.sent-2411-200: This process, known as encoding, maps the discrete tokens into a continuous high-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-201: Formally, this encoding can be expressed as a function $f: x \mapsto z_x$, where $x$ represents the original text and $z_x$ denotes its encoded representation in a high-dimensional vector space.
elements.sent-2411-202: The encoding process is designed to capture the semantic relationships and contextual information present in natural language.
elements.sent-2411-203: By projecting text into a high-dimensional space, these encodings enable machine learning models to discern subtle linguistic patterns and relationships that may not be immediately apparent in the original text.
elements.sent-2411-204: Several text encoders have been developed to perform this crucial transformation.
elements.sent-2411-205: Among the most prominent are T5~\citep{t5 , BERT~\citep{bert , and RoBERTa~\citep{roberta , each employing distinct architectural and training paradigms to generate rich, contextual representations of text:.}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: T5 (Text-To-Text Transfer Transformer)~\citep{t5: T5 represents a paradigm shift in natural language processing by recasting all NLP tasks within a unified text-to-text framework.}
elements.sent-2411-207: This approach enables T5 to address a diverse array of linguistic challenges, including but not limited to machine translation, text summarisation, and question answering.
elements.sent-2411-208: The architecture of T5 is predicated on a sequence-to-sequence model, which is trained to transmute input text into output text through a series of encoder and decoder transformations.
elements.sent-2411-209: This universal approach allows T5 to capture a wide spectrum of linguistic features and inter-textual relationships, making it exceptionally versatile for various NLP applications.
elements.sent-2411-210: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)~\citep{bert: BERT's innovation lies in its bidirectional training regime, which enables the model to comprehend the context of a word by considering both its preceding and subsequent context simultaneously.}
elements.sent-2411-211: This bidirectional approach represents a significant advancement over unidirectional language models.
elements.sent-2411-212: BERT's architecture is based on the transformer model, which utilises self-attention mechanisms to weigh the importance of different words in a sequence when computing representations.
elements.sent-2411-213: The result is a set of deep, contextual embeddings for each word, which can be fine-tuned for a multitude of downstream NLP tasks, including sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, and text classification.
elements.sent-2411-214: RoBERTa (A Robustly Optimised BERT Pretraining Approach) \citep{roberta: RoBERTa builds upon the foundational architecture of BERT while introducing several key optimisations to enhance its performance and robustness.}
elements.sent-2411-215: These enhancements include training on significantly larger datasets, removing the next sentence prediction objective, and using larger batch sizes.
elements.sent-2411-216: Additionally, RoBERTa utilises longer sequences during training, allowing it to capture long-range dependencies more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-217: These modifications collectively result in a more powerful and versatile language model, capable of producing highly nuanced and context-aware representations.
elements.sent-2411-218: These encoders are part of a broader evolution in text embedding methods, which has progressed from static embeddings, such as GloVe~\citep{glove and ELMo~~\citep{elmo , to dynamic contextualised embeddings introduced by models like BERT.}}
elements.sent-2411-219: As the scale of these models has increased, so too has their ability to capture complex patterns in text, although this comes with higher computational demands.
elements.sent-2411-220: In recent years, large language models (LLMs) have shifted towards decoder-only architectures, such as GPT.
elements.sent-2411-221: Despite this trend, in domains like music text analysis, models from the second generation of encoders—T5, BERT, RoBERTa—continue to play a central role, since they keep a balance in both effectiveness and performance within the context of music-text multimodal learning.
elements.sent-2411-222: These advanced text encoders transform raw text into dense vector representations that encapsulate semantic meanings, syntactic structures, and complex inter-word relationships.
elements.sent-2411-223: In the context of music generation, these sophisticated text representations can be leveraged to condition generative models, guiding the music creation process to align with specific textual descriptions or semantic concepts.
elements.sent-2411-224: The representation of music for machine learning applications presents unique challenges due to its multi-faceted nature, encompassing temporal, spectral, and structural dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-225: Music representations can be broadly categorised into two main classes: symbolic representations and audio representations.
elements.sent-2411-226: Each of these categories offers distinct advantages and is suited to different types of musical analysis and generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-227: Symbolic music representations encode musical information in a discrete, event-based format, abstracting away from the continuous nature of audio signals.
elements.sent-2411-228: These representations typically capture high-level musical concepts such as notes, chords, rhythm, and dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-229: The most prevalent format for symbolic music representation is the Musical Instrument Digital Interface (MIDI) protocol, which encodes musical events as a series of messages specifying pitches, note onsets, durations (by calculating time deltas between note on and off messages), velocities, and other performance parameters.
elements.sent-2411-230: Symbolic music representation has evolved significantly over time, starting with early formats like piano rolls, MusicXML, MIDI, and ABC notation.
elements.sent-2411-231: While these formats provided a foundation for digital music encoding, they also revealed limitations such as sparsity and difficulty in capturing nuanced musical details.
elements.sent-2411-232: Much like text tokenisation, where natural language is represented as sequences of discrete tokens, symbolic music representations face the challenge of converting non-sequential musical elements into serial forms that can be efficiently modelled.
elements.sent-2411-233: However, unlike language, where word order plays a dominant role, music inherently involves multiple, simultaneous streams of information (melody, harmony, rhythm), making its serialisation more complex and less straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-234: Early symbolic representations like piano rolls and MIDI often suffer from sparsity and quantisation issues, particularly when it comes to representing expressive performances.
elements.sent-2411-235: Recent research has sought to address these limitations by developing more sophisticated encoding schemes that aim for tighter, more structured representations of music.
elements.sent-2411-236: For example, models such as PianoTree VAE \citep{pianotreevae attempt to model music on a score level, focusing on hierarchical structures that reflect music's inherent nesting of notes within chords and phrases.}
elements.sent-2411-237: On the other hand, performance-based models like REMI~\citep{remi and CWT~\citep{cwt focus on capturing the nuances of performance, introducing tokens that encode rhythmic, harmonic, and temporal information.}}
elements.sent-2411-238: Despite these advancements, symbolic music tokenisation still faces inherent trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-239: REMI, for example, offers a more compact and structured encoding compared to MIDI, but it relies on fixed temporal grids, which can result in a loss of expressive timing and rhythmic precision.
elements.sent-2411-240: This quantisation problem, as noted by PerTok~\citep{pertok and other models, limits the ability to capture subtle performance details such as rubato and swing, which are crucial for realistic music generation.}
elements.sent-2411-241: Tokenizers like CWT and PerTok have emerged in response to this challenge, aiming to balance compactness with performance fidelity by modeling both local and global musical structures.
elements.sent-2411-242: While symbolic representations remain competitive in music processing tasks due to their compactness, editability, and interpretability, they have not dominated recent advances in music-text multimodal models.
elements.sent-2411-243: A key reason for this is the relative scarcity of symbolic music data compared to audio data.
elements.sent-2411-244: Audio-based approaches have benefited from the sheer volume of available data, which has driven recent breakthroughs in multimodal systems.
elements.sent-2411-245: This inherent limitation poses a challenge for the continued development of symbolic representations in the context of large-scale, data-driven machine learning models.
elements.sent-2411-246: Nevertheless, symbolic encoding remains a critical area of research for its advantages in specific tasks such as music generation, analysis, and manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-247: Audio representations deal directly with the continuous, time-varying pressure waves that constitute musical sounds.
elements.sent-2411-248: While raw audio waveforms contain the full fidelity of a musical signal, their length and complex temporal structure make them challenging to process directly with many machine learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-249: Consequently, various transformations and feature extraction techniques are employed to convert raw audio into more tractable representations that capture salient musical characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-250: Time-frequency representations aim to capture both the temporal evolution and spectral content of audio signals, providing a rich description of musical timbre, harmony, and rhythm.
elements.sent-2411-251: Spectrogram: A spectrogram is a visual representation of the spectrum of frequencies in a sound or other signal as they vary with time.
elements.sent-2411-252: It is typically computed using the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT), which applies the Fourier transform to windowed segments of the audio signal.
elements.sent-2411-253: The resulting spectrogram $S(t,f)$ represents the magnitude of the STFT, where $t$ denotes time and $f$ denotes frequency.
elements.sent-2411-254: Mathematically, the STFT can be expressed as: \begin{equation}STFT\{x(t)\}(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)w(t-\tau)e^{-j\omega t}dt,\end{equation} where $x(t)$ is the input signal, $w(t)$ is the window function, $\tau$ is the time shift, and $\omega$ is the angular frequency.
elements.sent-2411-255: Spectrograms offer a detailed view of the frequency content of a signal over time, making them useful for tasks such as instrument recognition, onset detection, and audio source separation.
elements.sent-2411-256: Mel-spectrogram: A mel-spectrogram is a variant of the spectrogram that applies a mel-scale filterbank to the power spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-257: The mel scale is a perceptual scale of pitches judged by listeners to be equal in distance from one another.
elements.sent-2411-258: This transformation aligns the frequency representation more closely with human auditory perception.
elements.sent-2411-259: The conversion from Hz to mel can be approximated by: \begin{equation}m = 2595 \log_{10}(1 + \frac{f}{700}),\end{equation} where $f$ is the frequency in Hz and $m$ is the corresponding mel value.
elements.sent-2411-260: Mel-spectrograms are widely used in music information retrieval tasks due to their ability to capture perceptually relevant features of music while reducing the dimensionality of the representation.
elements.sent-2411-261: Mel-frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs): MFCCs are a compact representation of the short-term power spectrum of a sound, based on a linear cosine transform of a log power spectrum on a nonlinear mel scale of frequency.
elements.sent-2411-262: The process of computing MFCCs involves: \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-263: Computing the power spectrum of windowed frames of the audio signal.
elements.sent-2411-264: Applying a mel-scale filterbank to the power spectra.
elements.sent-2411-265: Taking the logarithm of the filterbank energies.
elements.sent-2411-266: Computing the discrete cosine transform (DCT) of the log filterbank energies.
elements.sent-2411-267: MFCCs are particularly effective at capturing the timbral characteristics of sound and have been widely used in speech recognition, music genre classification, and instrument identification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-268: From an informatics perspective, audio encoders efficiently compress audio data into rich, task-agnostic representations, enabling powerful downstream music analysis and generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-269: Earlier models such as Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)~\citep{vae and WaveNet~\citep{wavenet laid the groundwork for compact representations, while large-scale pretrained models like Jukebox~\citep{jukebox have demonstrated the ability to retain domain-specific knowledge.}}}
elements.sent-2411-270: When probed using a simple two-layer MLP, these models reveal that their large-scale training allows them to capture a wealth of useful information for various tasks.
elements.sent-2411-271: Audio encoders have developed along two main lines: one leverages prior knowledge of musical theory, while the other focuses on scaling up model parameters to gain advantages in performance.
elements.sent-2411-272: The latter approach, which relies on sheer model size and training data, has recently achieved greater success.
elements.sent-2411-273: The following models represent the latest advances in this field, each contributing to audio representation learning through different architectures and techniques.
elements.sent-2411-274: HTS-AT (Hierarchical Token-Semantic Audio Transformer) \citep{htsat: HTS-AT is a transformer-based model designed specifically for sound classification and detection.}
elements.sent-2411-276: A hierarchical structure to reduce model size and training time.
elements.sent-2411-277: A token-semantic module for mapping final outputs into class feature maps, enabling audio event detection and localisation.
elements.sent-2411-278: PaSST (Patchout Fast Spectrogram Transformer)~\citep{passt: PaSST applies a novel training method to transformers on audio spectrograms.}
elements.sent-2411-279: The key innovations of PaSST include: \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-280: Patch extraction and linear projection to form a sequence for the transformer.
elements.sent-2411-281: Patchout, a method to reduce computation and memory complexity during training, also functioning as a regulariser.
elements.sent-2411-282: MERT (Music undERstanding model with large-scale self-supervised Training)~\citep{mert: MERT is a model that incorporates teacher models to provide pseudo labels in a masked language modelling style acoustic pre-training.}
elements.sent-2411-284: A combination of an acoustic teacher based on Residual Vector Quantisation Variational AutoEncoder (RVQ-VAE)~\citep{rvq and a musical teacher based on the ConstantQ Transform (CQT).}
elements.sent-2411-285: A multi-task paradigm balancing acoustic and musical representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-286: While there are no clear superior or inferior models in this space, each of these modern audio encoders finds its own niche in different tasks, and they coexist in parallel, contributing to the broader landscape of audio representation learning.
elements.subsubsection-2411-003: Cross-Modal Representation: CLAP and Its Variants
elements.sent-2411-287: The increasing interest in cross-modal learning has led to the development of models that can jointly process and align representations from different modalities.
elements.sent-2411-288: One of the most representative models in this domain is CLAP (Contrastive Language-Audio Pretraining), introduced by \citet{clap , which extends the principles of CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining)~\citep{clip to the audio domain, enabling joint representation learning of music and text.}}
elements.sent-2411-289: Multimodal alignment tasks inherently deal with the heterogeneity between different modalities, such as text and audio, which represent information in fundamentally different ways.
elements.sent-2411-290: Cross-modal machine learning aims to resolve this by aligning the representations of these distinct modalities into a shared space, where they can be processed together more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-291: In the context of this thesis, which focuses on text-to-music generation, cross-modal representations offer an improved framework for linking text-based inputs with music outputs, ultimately allowing for better multimodal integration.
elements.sent-2411-293: Dual Encoder Architecture: CLAP employs separate encoders for processing audio and text inputs.
elements.sent-2411-294: The audio encoder is typically based on a convolutional or transformer architecture, while the text encoder utilises pretrained language models such as BERT or RoBERTa.
elements.sent-2411-295: Contrastive Learning Objective: CLAP is trained using a contrastive learning approach, where the model learns to maximise the similarity between embeddings of matching audio-text pairs while minimising the similarity between non-matching pairs.
elements.sent-2411-296: Large-Scale Pretraining: CLAP is pretrained on large datasets (LAION-630K) \footnote{LAION-630K dataset: \url{https://github.com/LAION-AI/audio-dataset/tree/main/laion-audio-630k .}}
elements.sent-2411-297: The contrastive learning objective of CLAP can be formalised as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = -\log \frac{\exp(sim(f_a(x_i), f_t(y_i)) / \tau)}{\sum_{j} \exp(sim(f_a(x_i), f_t(y_j)) / \tau)},\end{equation} where $f_a$ and $f_t$ are the audio and text encoders respectively, $x_i$ and $y_i$ are corresponding audio and text inputs, $sim$ is a similarity function (typically cosine similarity), and $\tau$ is a temperature parameter.
elements.sent-2411-298: Although CLAP is one of the most representative multimodal models, it is not the only one in the field.
elements.sent-2411-299: Several variants and other approaches~\citep{compa, clapnew have recently emerged, each pushing the performance of multimodal alignment models further.}
elements.sent-2411-300: The development of CLAP and its successors represents a significant step towards achieving unified multimodal understanding of music and language, with applications ranging from music cognition to creative AI applications.
elements.sent-2411-301: In the context of text-to-music generation, these models offer a more coherent way to align text and music modalities, addressing the inherent heterogeneity in multimodal tasks and driving forward the potential for cross-modal translation and generation.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Pretraining, Continued Pretraining, Post-training, and Finetuning
elements.sent-2411-302: The processes of pretraining, continued pretraining, post-training, and finetuning form a cohesive workflow commonly used in large-scale model training.
elements.sent-2411-303: Each phase builds on the previous one to incrementally refine the model's versatility and task-specific performance.
elements.sent-2411-304: While continued pretraining is less frequently applied, it can significantly enhance a model's ability to handle complex or specialised data.
elements.sent-2411-305: Post-training and finetuning further optimise the model for adaptability and precision, ensuring its effectiveness in specific applications.
elements.sent-2411-306: Pretraining: This is the initial phase where a model is trained on a vast and varied dataset to discern general patterns and extract fundamental features.
elements.sent-2411-307: In our research, I concentrate on pretraining text-to-music models to grasp the intrinsic connections between textual inputs and their musical counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-308: This foundational step utilises an extensive corpus of paired text and music to construct a versatile base model that can interpret and produce music from textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-309: Continued Pretraining: Following the initial pretraining, continued pretraining involves further training on additional data to enhance the model's generalisation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-310: Although not that common, this step is helpful to strengthen the model's understanding with more data.
elements.sent-2411-311: Post-training: Post-training is a transitional phase where the model undergoes adjustments and optimisations based on the insights gained from pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-312: This step might involve techniques such as knowledge distillation or model compression, aiming to make the model more efficient or suitable for deployment in specific environments.
elements.sent-2411-313: Finetuning: The final stage involves finetuning the model on a smaller, task-specific dataset.
elements.sent-2411-314: This step tailors the model to meet specific requirements, such as generating music that adheres to particular styles or incorporating user-defined constraints.
elements.sent-2411-315: Finetuning often introduces new control mechanisms, enabling the model to generate music that is more aligned with detailed textual descriptions and additional user inputs.
elements.sent-2411-316: Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT): As a subset of finetuning, SFT focuses on further honing the model's performance under the guidance of labeled data.
elements.sent-2411-317: This supervised approach ensures that the model not only adapts to the task but also maintains a high level of accuracy and consistency in its outputs.
elements.sent-2411-318: In this section, I will explore the backbone models that are fundamental to this thesis in text-to-music generation.
elements.sent-2411-319: These models provide the underlying architecture and mechanisms through which text inputs are transformed into music outputs.
elements.sent-2411-320: DDPMs~\citep{ddpm operate by gradually adding noise to the data and then learning to reverse this process, effectively denoising the data to generate new samples.}
elements.sent-2411-321: The model is trained on a forward diffusion process that progressively adds Gaussian noise to the data, and a reverse process that learns to denoise the data.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/ddpm.pdf} \caption{The forward and backward process of the diffusion model.} \label{fig:ddpm}
elements.sent-2411-322: The forward process \( q \) adds noise to the data \( y \) over \( T \) time steps: \begin{equation}q(y_t | y_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(y_t; \sqrt{\alpha_t} y_{t-1}, (1 - \alpha_t). \mathbf{I}),\end{equation} where \( \alpha_t \) is a schedule parameter that controls the variance of the noise added at each step.
elements.sent-2411-324: The term \( \sqrt{\alpha_t y_{t-1 \) represents the mean of the noisy data at step \( t \), and \( (1 - \alpha_t) \mathbf{I \) represents the variance, which is controlled by \( \alpha_t \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-325: This process adds a small amount of noise at each step.
elements.sent-2411-326: This process can be iteratively applied starting from the original data \( y_0 \): \begin{equation}q(y_{1:T} | y_0) = \prod_{t=1}^T q(y_t | y_{t-1}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-327: After enough steps, \( y_T \) becomes almost pure noise.
elements.sent-2411-328: The idea is that by the end of the forward process, the data should be sufficiently noisy, resembling a Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-329: The reverse process \( p \) aims to denoise \( y \) by estimating the original data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-330: It is defined as: \begin{equation}p_\theta(y_{t-1} | y_t) = \mathcal{N}(y_{t-1}; \mu_\theta(y_t, t), \Sigma_\theta(y_t, t)),\end{equation} where \( \mu_\theta(y_t, t) \) and \( \Sigma_\theta(y_t, t) \) are parameterised by neural networks and are learned during training.
elements.sent-2411-331: In the reverse process, the model predicts the previous step \( y_{t-1 \) from the current step \( y_t \).}
elements.sent-2411-332: The mean \( \mu_\theta \) and the variance \( \Sigma_\theta \) are learned functions that depend on \( y_t \) and the time step \( t \).
elements.sent-2411-333: The reverse process can be written as: \begin{equation}p_\theta(y_{0:T}) = p(y_T) \prod_{t=1}^T p_\theta(y_{t-1} | y_t).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-334: This represents the application of the reverse process over \( T \) steps, starting from almost pure noise \( y_T \).
elements.sent-2411-335: The reverse process is essentially a learned denoising pathway that recovers the original data distribution from the noisy version.
elements.sent-2411-336: The training objective is to minimise the difference between the forward and reverse processes, which can be expressed as a variational lower bound: \begin{equation}L = \mathbb{E}_{q} \left[ \sum_{t=1}^T D_\text{KL}(q(y_{t-1} | y_t, y_0) | p_\theta(y_{t-1} | y_t)) \right],\end{equation} where \( D_\text{KL \) denotes the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the true forward process and the learned reverse process.}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}L = E_{q} \left[ \sum_{t=1}^T D_KL(q(y_{t-1} | y_t, y_0) | p_\theta(y_{t-1} | y_t)) \right],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-337: The objective function \( L \) is the expected sum of the Kullback-Leibler divergences between the true distribution and the predicted distribution at each step.
elements.sent-2411-338: Minimising this loss function trains the model to accurately reverse the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-339: Latent diffusion models~\citep{ldm build on the principles of DDPM but operate in a latent space rather than directly on the data space.}
elements.sent-2411-340: This approach can reduce computational complexity and improve efficiency, making it particularly suitable for high-dimensional data like music.
elements.sent-2411-341: The key advantage of LDMs is that they handle the diffusion process in a compressed representation of the data, which significantly reduces the computational burden while preserving the essential characteristics of the data.
elements.sent-2411-342: In latent diffusion models, the data \( y \) is first mapped to a lower-dimensional latent space \( z \) using an encoder: \begin{equation}z = E(y).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-343: The encoder \( E \) compresses the high-dimensional data \( y \) into a lower-dimensional latent representation \( z \), which retains the essential features of the original data.
elements.sent-2411-344: After the diffusion process is applied in the latent space, the denoised latent variables are mapped back to the data space using a decoder: \begin{equation}\hat{y} = D(z).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-345: The decoder \( D \) reconstructs the data \( \hat{y \) from the denoised latent variables \( z \), producing an output that is similar to the original data.}
elements.paragraph-2411-009: Forward and Reverse Processes in Latent Space.
elements.sent-2411-346: The forward process \( q \) and reverse process \( p \) in the latent space are defined similarly to DDPM: \begin{equation}q(z_t | z_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(z_t; \sqrt{\alpha_t} z_{t-1}, (1 - \alpha_t) \mathbf{I})\end{equation} The forward process in the latent space adds Gaussian noise to the latent variables \( z \) at each step, similar to the forward process in DDPM but operating on the compressed representation.. \begin{equation}p_\theta(z_{t-1} | z_t) = \mathcal{N}(z_{t-1}; \mu_\theta(z_t, t), \Sigma_\theta(z_t, t)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-347: The reverse process in the latent space predicts the previous latent variable \( z_{t-1 \) from the current latent variable \( z_t \), using learned functions \( \mu_\theta \) and \( \Sigma_\theta \).}
elements.sent-2411-348: The objective remains to minimise the difference between the forward and reverse processes, but this time in the latent space: \begin{equation}L = \mathbb{E}_{q} \left[ \sum_{t=1}^T D_{KL}(q(z_{t-1} | z_t, z_0) | p_\theta(z_{t-1} | z_t)) \right].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}L = E_{q} \left[ \sum_{t=1}^T D_{KL}(q(z_{t-1} | z_t, z_0) | p_\theta(z_{t-1} | z_t)) \right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-349: The training objective is to minimise the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the true forward process and the learned reverse process in the latent space, ensuring accurate reconstruction of the original data from the latent variables.
elements.subsubsection-2411-006: Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) and Fast Sampling
elements.sent-2411-350: Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIMs)~\citep{ddim enhance the efficiency of sampling in diffusion models by introducing a deterministic alternative to the stochastic reverse process found in Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs).}
elements.sent-2411-351: This deterministic approach allows for faster sampling while maintaining the quality of the generated outputs.
elements.sent-2411-352: DDPMs require many steps to gradually denoise data, which can be computationally expensive and slow.
elements.sent-2411-353: DDIMs address this limitation by modifying the reverse process to follow a deterministic path, eliminating the need for sampling from a Gaussian distribution at each step.
elements.sent-2411-354: This deterministic reverse process can be described as: \begin{equation}y_{t-1} = \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}} \left( \frac{y_t - \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t} \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t)}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}} \right) + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_{t-1}} \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-355: In this equation, \( \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t) \) represents the predicted noise component at step \( t \), obtained from a neural network, and \( \alpha_t \) is the noise schedule parameter.
elements.sent-2411-356: By following this deterministic path, DDIMs can generate samples with fewer steps compared to the stochastic sampling of DDPMs.
elements.sent-2411-357: To understand DDIMs in detail, it is crucial to grasp several key concepts:.
elements.sent-2411-358: Noise Schedule Parameter (\( \alpha_t \)): This parameter controls the amount of noise added at each step of the forward diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-359: It typically follows a schedule that starts with large values and gradually decreases, allowing the model to add less noise as it progresses.
elements.sent-2411-360: This schedule is critical in ensuring that the model can effectively learn the denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-361: Predictive Noise Model (\( \epsilon_\theta \)): The neural network \( \epsilon_\theta \) is trained to predict the noise that was added to the data at each step.
elements.sent-2411-362: Deterministic Reverse Process: Unlike the stochastic nature of DDPMs, where each step involves sampling from a Gaussian distribution, DDIMs use a deterministic formula to revert the noisy data back to its original state.
elements.sent-2411-363: This deterministic nature reduces the variability and randomness in the generated samples, leading to more consistent outputs.
elements.sent-2411-364: To implement DDIMs, the training phase involves a forward process that progressively adds Gaussian noise to the data, defined by: \begin{equation}q(y_t | y_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(y_t; \sqrt{\alpha_t} y_{t-1}, (1 - \alpha_t) \mathbf{I}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-365: A neural network \( \epsilon_\theta \) is trained to predict the noise added at each step by minimising the difference between the predicted noise and the actual noise: \begin{equation}L_{train} = \mathbb{E}_{q} \left[ | \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t) - \epsilon |^2 \right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-366: During sampling, the trained model applies the deterministic reverse process, starting from pure noise \( y_T \) and iteratively generating the final sample \( y_0 \).
elements.sent-2411-367: For text-to-music generation, text embeddings are incorporated into the neural network \( \epsilon_\theta \), conditioning the generation process on the text input.
elements.sent-2411-368: The modified reverse process becomes: \begin{equation}y_{t-1} = \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}} \left( \frac{y_t - \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t} \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t, x)}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}} \right) + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_{t-1}} \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t, x).\end{equation}.
elements.subsubsection-2411-007: Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) Interpretation of DDPMs
elements.sent-2411-369: The forward diffusion process in DDPMs can be viewed through the lens of a Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE).
elements.sent-2411-370: An SDE provides a continuous-time perspective on the discrete-time forward diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-371: In this framework, the forward process is seen as a continuous-time stochastic process governed by the SDE: \begin{equation}dX_t = -\frac{1}{2}\beta(t) X_t dt + \sqrt{\beta(t)} dW_t,\end{equation} where \(X_t\) is the state of the system at time \(t\), \(\beta(t)\) is a time-dependent diffusion coefficient, and \(W_t\) is a Wiener process representing the source of random noise.
elements.sent-2411-372: This equation describes how noise is continuously added to the data over time, leading to a gradual loss of information.
elements.subsubsection-2411-008: Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) Interpretation of DDIMs
elements.sent-2411-373: DDIMs, on the other hand, offer a deterministic counterpart to the stochastic diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-374: Instead of an SDE, the reverse process in DDIMs can be interpreted as an Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE), which governs a deterministic trajectory from noise back to the original data: \begin{equation}\frac{dX_t}{dt} = -\frac{1}{2}\beta(t) X_t + \sqrt{\beta(t)} \epsilon_\theta(X_t, t),\end{equation} where \(\epsilon_\theta(X_t, t)\) is the predicted noise component at time \(t\) obtained from the trained neural network.
elements.sent-2411-375: This ODE formulation allows for fast and deterministic sampling, as it removes the stochasticity present in the reverse process of DDPMs.
elements.subsubsection-2411-009: Connecting DDPMs and DDIMs Through SDE and ODE
elements.sent-2411-376: The SDE interpretation of DDPMs and the ODE interpretation of DDIMs highlight the transition from a stochastic to a deterministic framework.
elements.sent-2411-377: Both models start with the same goal: to denoise data that has been corrupted by additive Gaussian noise.
elements.sent-2411-378: However, they achieve this goal through different methods.
elements.sent-2411-379: In DDPMs, the SDE provides a probabilistic framework that captures the inherent uncertainty in the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-380: The reverse process in DDPMs is inherently stochastic, aiming to estimate the posterior distribution of the data at each step.
elements.sent-2411-381: This stochastic nature leads to slower sampling as each step requires a draw from a probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-382: In contrast, DDIMs employ an ODE to guide the reverse process deterministically.
elements.sent-2411-383: By removing the stochastic component, DDIMs can significantly speed up the sampling process without compromising the quality of the generated outputs.
elements.sent-2411-384: This deterministic approach makes DDIMs more efficient for applications requiring real-time or rapid generation.
elements.sent-2411-385: The fast sampling in DDIMs is achieved by utilising the deterministic reverse process derived from the ODE.
elements.sent-2411-386: This process can be summarised as: \begin{equation}X_{t-\Delta t} = X_t - \frac{1}{2}\beta(t) X_t \Delta t + \sqrt{\beta(t)} \epsilon_\theta(X_t, t) \Delta t,\end{equation} where \(\Delta t\) is the time step size.
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}X_{t-\Delta t} = X_t - \frac{1}{2}\beta(t) X_t \Delta t + \sqrt{\beta(t)} \epsilon_\theta(X_t, t) \Delta t,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-387: By choosing appropriate values for \(\Delta t\), the number of steps required for sampling can be reduced, leading to faster generation times.
elements.subsubsection-2411-011: DPM-Solver: A Fast ODE Solver for Diffusion Probabilistic Model Sampling
elements.sent-2411-388: Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DPMs) typically require a large number of steps for accurate sampling, which can be slow and computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-389: DPM-Solver~\citep{dpmsolver is an efficient method that addresses this issue by using a fast Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) solver, significantly reducing the number of steps required for sampling.}
elements.sent-2411-390: DPM-Solver leverages the fact that the reverse diffusion process can be viewed as solving an ODE.
elements.sent-2411-391: By applying advanced ODE solvers, DPM-Solver can generate high-quality samples in as few as ten steps.
elements.sent-2411-392: The core idea is to approximate the reverse diffusion process using a discretised ODE, which can be solved efficiently using numerical methods.
elements.sent-2411-393: The forward diffusion process can be described by the following Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE): \begin{equation}d\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{y}, t) dt + g(t) d\mathbf{w},\end{equation} where \( \mathbf{y \) is the data, \( f \) and \( g \) are functions defining the drift and diffusion coefficients, and \( d\mathbf{w \) represents the Wiener process.}}
elements.sent-2411-394: The reverse process, which aims to denoise the data, can be reformulated as an ODE: \begin{equation}d\mathbf{y} = \left( f(\mathbf{y}, t) - g(t)^2 \nabla_{\mathbf{y}} \log p_t(\mathbf{y}) \right) dt.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-395: In this formulation, \( f(\mathbf{y , t) \) captures the deterministic part of the process, while \( g(t)^2 \nabla_{\mathbf{y \log p_t(\mathbf{y ) \) represents the gradient of the log probability, which acts as a correction term to guide the data back to its original distribution.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-396: DPM-Solver approximates this ODE using advanced numerical solvers, such as Runge-Kutta methods, which can efficiently handle the discretisation of the reverse process.
elements.sent-2411-397: These solvers can adapt the step size dynamically, ensuring that the approximation remains accurate while minimising the computational burden.
elements.sent-2411-398: In practice, DPM-Solver can be integrated into existing diffusion models by replacing the standard reverse diffusion process with the ODE-based solver.
elements.sent-2411-399: This integration results in faster sampling times and reduced computational costs, making it suitable for real-time applications like text-to-music generation.
elements.sent-2411-400: Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIMs) introduce an efficient deterministic sampling method for diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-401: A particularly useful feature of DDIMs is their ability to invert the generative process, which means one can map generated samples back to latent variables.
elements.sent-2411-402: This inversion is crucial for applications like text-to-music generation, where conditioning on text input must be precise and controllable.
elements.sent-2411-403: DDIM inversion is the process of mapping generated samples \( \hat{y _0 \) back to their latent representations \( z \).}
elements.sent-2411-404: This inversion allows for editing or modifying the generated samples by manipulating their latent representations and then re-generating the samples using the reverse DDIM process.
elements.sent-2411-405: Given the deterministic nature of DDIMs, the inversion process can be described as follows.
elements.sent-2411-406: The forward process starts from the initial data \( y_0 \) and adds noise according to the schedule parameter \( \alpha_t \): \begin{equation}q(y_t | y_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(y_t; \sqrt{\alpha_t} y_{t-1}, (1 - \alpha_t) \mathbf{I}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-407: In the reverse process, using the learned neural network \( \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t) \), the deterministic reverse process predicts \( y_{t-1 \) from \( y_t \): \begin{equation}y_{t-1} = \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}} \left( \frac{y_t - \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t} \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t)}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}} \right) + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_{t-1}} \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-408: To invert the generative process, it is needed to estimate the latent variable \( z \) that corresponds to the generated sample \( \hat{y _0 \).}
elements.sent-2411-409: This involves applying the forward diffusion process in reverse: \begin{equation}z_t = \frac{\hat{y}_0 - \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t} \epsilon_\theta(\hat{y}_0, t)}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}}\end{equation} This equation estimates the latent variable \( z_t \) from the generated sample \( \hat{y _0 \) by removing the noise component predicted by the neural network.}
elements.sent-2411-410: Text inversion in the context of DDIMs involves incorporating text embeddings into the diffusion model to guide the generation process.
elements.sent-2411-411: This conditioning allows for more accurate and contextually relevant generation, particularly in tasks like text-to-music generation.
elements.sent-2411-412: To incorporate text \( x \) into the DDIM framework, I use text embeddings \( \phi(x) \), which are integrated into the neural network \( \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t, \phi(x)) \).
elements.sent-2411-413: This conditioning ensures that the generated samples align with the textual input.
elements.sent-2411-414: First, represent the text \( x \) using a pretrained text encoder, producing embeddings \( \phi(x) \): \begin{equation}\phi(x) = \text{TextEncoder}(x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-415: Next, modify the noise prediction network to incorporate text embeddings, producing a conditioned noise estimate: \begin{equation}\epsilon_\theta(y_t, t, \phi(x)) = \text{NeuralNetwork}(y_t, t, \phi(x)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-416: Finally, integrate the text-conditioned noise prediction into the reverse process: \begin{equation}y_{t-1} = \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}} \left( \frac{y_t - \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t} \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t, \phi(x))}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}} \right) + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_{t-1}} \epsilon_\theta(y_t, t, \phi(x)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-417: This modified reverse process uses text embeddings to influence the denoising steps, producing samples that reflect the input text \( x \).
elements.sent-2411-418: The inversion process with text conditioning involves estimating the latent variables that correspond to a generated sample conditioned on text input.
elements.sent-2411-419: This can be achieved by incorporating the text embeddings into the inversion equation: \begin{equation}z_t = \frac{\hat{y}_0 - \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t} \epsilon_\theta(\hat{y}_0, t, \phi(x))}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-420: This formula estimates the latent variable \( z_t \) from the generated sample \( \hat{y _0 \) and the text-conditioned noise prediction \( \epsilon_\theta(\hat{y _0, t, \phi(x)) \).}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-013: Related Work on Diffusion-Based Text-to-Music Backbone Models
elements.sent-2411-421: The evolution of diffusion-based text-to-music models reflects a series of incremental innovations and refinements, each building on the strengths of its predecessors while addressing specific limitations.
elements.sent-2411-422: These models can be grouped into several categories based on their shared architectures, conditioners, and improvements in output quality.
elements.sent-2411-423: Table~\ref{tab:diffusion_list provides an overview of these models.}
elements.sent-2411-424: The journey began with Riffusion (2022), which applied diffusion processes to music using methods initially designed for image generation, without making specific optimisations for musical outputs.
elements.sent-2411-425: This set the stage for subsequent models like Mousai (2023) and Noise2Music (2023), which moved beyond Riffusion by employing cascaded 1D UNet architectures, specifically conditioned on T5, to generate longer, higher-quality waveforms.
elements.sent-2411-426: These models demonstrated a significant step forward by directly targeting music generation with dedicated architectures.
elements.sent-2411-427: In the same period, TANGO (2023) and its refined version, Mustango (2023), emerged, sharing the same VAE and 2D UNet architecture while utilising the FLAN-T5 conditioner.
elements.sent-2411-428: Mustango introduced subtle improvements over TANGO, maintaining the same core framework but optimising performance and generation efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-429: Similarly, the AudioLDM family, consisting of Audio\-LDM, MusicLDM, and AudioLDM 2, followed a trajectory of continuous refinement.
elements.sent-2411-430: MusicLDM is essentially a fine-tuned version of AudioLDM, while AudioLDM 2 brought separate improvements specifically to the encoder, enhancing flexibility and adaptability across various tasks.
elements.sent-2411-431: Meanwhile, Stable Audio (2024) and Stable Audio 2 further advanced the field.
elements.sent-2411-432: Stable Audio employed a VAE and 1D UNet architecture conditioned on CLAP, generating up to 95-second audio outputs.
elements.sent-2411-433: Stable Audio 2 extended this capability by introducing DiT (Diffusion Transformers), significantly increasing the length of generated outputs to up to 4 minutes and 45 seconds, marking a notable leap in generation length and quality.
elements.sent-2411-434: Lastly, Jen-1 and Jen-1 Composer (2023) introduced more sophisticated features, including support for multitrack generation in Jen-1 Composer, built on the foundation of MAE and 1D UNet architectures.
elements.sent-2411-435: These models leveraged FLAN-T5 for conditioning, focusing on producing high-quality stereo waveforms, with Jen-1 Composer expanding capabilities to multitrack outputs for more complex compositions.
elements.sent-2411-436: Riffusion~\citep{riffusion is one of the pioneering projects in the field of text-to-music generation using diffusion models.}
elements.sent-2411-437: The core component of Riffusion is its use of a latent text-to-image diffusion model, specifically a fine-tuned Stable-Diffusion-v1-5 checkpoint, to create spectrograms from text.
elements.sent-2411-438: These spectrograms visually represent the audio and are subsequently converted into sound using the Griffin-Lim algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-439: This method allows for real-time music generation, showcasing the potential of diffusion models in audio synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-440: However, the reliance on Griffin-Lim limits the audio quality waveform reconstruction; the length of audio is also limited to only 5 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-441: Riffusion was trained on a dataset composed of paired text and spectrogram images, leveraging the LAION-5B dataset for text-image pairs and additional audio data.
elements.sent-2411-442: Despite its foundational nature and certain limitations in audio fidelity, Riffusion has significantly influenced subsequent research by demonstrating the feasibility of using diffusion models for generating music from textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-443: The project is available for public use and experimentation through its web app and model repository.
elements.sent-2411-444: Moûsai~\citep{mousai is another pioneering research model in the field of text-to-music generation, marking the first significant exploration into diffusion-based methods for this purpose.}
elements.sent-2411-445: It bridges the gap between textual descriptions and music by employing a highly efficient, expressive, and long-context handling approach.
elements.sent-2411-446: Moûsai leverages a cascading two-stage latent diffusion model that can generate high-quality stereo music at 48kHz, spanning multiple minutes from textual inputs.
elements.sent-2411-447: The core component of Moûsai is its diffusion autoencoder, which first compresses the audio into a magnitude-only spectrogram and then decodes it back to waveform using a custom 1D U-Net.
elements.sent-2411-448: This structure enables the model to efficiently handle long-term musical structure and produce real-time music generation on consumer GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-449: The dataset used for training Moûsai includes a large collection of high-quality music samples paired with textual descriptions, facilitating the model's capability to generate music that reflects the provided text.
elements.sent-2411-450: Noise2Music~\citep{noise2music is one of the pioneering models in the field of text-to-music generation, particularly notable for its use of diffusion models to directly generate audio waveforms from textual descriptions.}
elements.sent-2411-451: This model addresses the challenge of generating high-fidelity music by employing a two-stage diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-452: The first stage involves a generator model that produces an intermediate representation conditioned on the text input, while the second stage involves a cascader model that enhances the fidelity of this intermediate representation to produce high-quality audio.
elements.sent-2411-453: The core component of Noise2Music is its diffusion-based architecture, which leverages a 1D Efficient U-Net for the generator and cascader models.
elements.sent-2411-454: The generator model first generates a low-fidelity audio waveform at 3.2kHz conditioned on the text.
elements.sent-2411-455: This low-fidelity audio is then upsampled and refined by the cascader model to produce a high-fidelity audio waveform at 16kHz.
elements.sent-2411-456: This two-stage approach allows the model to effectively compress and refine audio information, leading to improved audio quality.
elements.sent-2411-457: For its training, Noise2Music utilises a large-scale dataset that includes MusicCaps~\citep{musiclm , AudioSet~\citep{audiocaps , and MagnaTagATune~\citep{mtat .}}}
elements.sent-2411-458: These datasets provide a diverse range of music samples paired with detailed textual descriptions, enabling the model to learn complex mappings between text and music.
elements.sent-2411-459: The model also employs pretrained large language models to generate paired text for the audio in the training set and to extract text embeddings for conditioning the diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-460: TANGO~\citep{tango is a versatile text-to-audio generation model that leverages a latent diffusion model (LDM) to generate high-quality audio, including music, from textual descriptions.}
elements.sent-2411-461: Initially designed for general text-to-audio tasks, TANGO was later adapted to include music generation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-462: The core component of TANGO is its use of the instruction-tuned large language model FLAN-T5~\citep{flant5 as the text encoder, which generates detailed text embeddings from input descriptions.}
elements.sent-2411-463: These embeddings are then used to guide the LDM in constructing a latent audio representation.
elements.sent-2411-464: The model utilises a 1D U-Net architecture within the LDM for both the forward and reverse diffusion processes, enabling it to handle the complexities of audio generation effectively.
elements.sent-2411-465: TANGO employs a cascading approach for diffusion, with a generator model producing an intermediate audio representation at a lower fidelity (e.g., 3.2kHz), which is then refined by a cascader model to a higher fidelity (e.g., 16kHz).
elements.sent-2411-466: This two-stage process helps in compressing and then enhancing the audio quality, ensuring that the final output is both high-fidelity and true to the textual prompt.
elements.sent-2411-467: Mustango~\citep{mustango extends the capabilities of the TANGO model by incorporating music-specific metadata to enhance controllability over the generated music.}
elements.sent-2411-468: This model allows users to provide detailed musical instructions in addition to general text descriptions, enabling control over aspects such as chords, beats, tempo, and key.
elements.sent-2411-469: The core component of Mustango is its use of MuNet, a Music-Domain-Knowledge-Informed UNet module.
elements.sent-2411-470: MuNet integrates these music-specific features, predicted from the text prompt, into the diffusion denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-471: This approach ensures that the generated music adheres to the specified musical properties, offering a high degree of control over the output.
elements.sent-2411-472: For training, TANGO utilises datasets such as AudioCaps, which contain extensive audio clips paired with human-written captions.
elements.sent-2411-473: Despite training on a relatively smaller dataset compared to other models, TANGO achieves impressive performance, owing to its robust architecture and the efficiency of its instruction-tuned text encoder.
elements.sent-2411-474: AudioLDM \citep{audioldm and MusicLDM \citep{musicldm are innovative models in the field of text-to-audio and text-to-music generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-475: Both models leverage Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) and the Contrastive Language-Audio Pretraining (CLAP) framework to bridge the gap between text and audio representations.
elements.sent-2411-476: Here I discuss each model and compare their approaches.
elements.sent-2411-477: AudioLDM generates audio from textual descriptions using a diffusion model operating in a latent space.
elements.sent-2411-478: It aims to create high-fidelity audio samples by leveraging the pretrained CLAP model to align text and audio embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-479: The core of AudioLDM involves two main components: the CLAP model and the Latent Diffusion Model (LDM).
elements.sent-2411-480: The CLAP model provides a shared embedding space for both text and audio.
elements.sent-2411-481: During training, audio embeddings from the CLAP model are used to condition the LDM.
elements.sent-2411-482: This conditioning is achieved through a Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) mechanism, which modulates the latent diffusion process using the text embeddings during inference.
elements.sent-2411-483: This approach allows the model to learn from audio-only data during training and use text embeddings for generation.
elements.sent-2411-484: AudioLDM is trained on a variety of datasets, including AudioSet and FSD50K, which provide diverse audio samples paired with textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-485: The model benefits from a large amount of audio data to learn robust audio representations.
elements.sent-2411-486: MusicLDM builds on the architecture of AudioLDM but introduces two significant modifications to enhance its performance for music generation.
elements.sent-2411-487: First, it retrains the CLAP model on text-music pair datasets to improve its understanding of musical contexts.
elements.sent-2411-488: Second, MusicLDM employs beat-synchronous mixup strategies for data augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-489: These strategies involve mixing audio samples at synchronised beats, ensuring the resultant training data is musically coherent.
elements.sent-2411-490: This beat-synchronous augmentation helps the model generate more diverse and rhythmically accurate music.
elements.sent-2411-491: MusicLDM is trained on specialised music datasets such as MusicCaps and MusicBench.
elements.sent-2411-492: These datasets contain rich textual descriptions of music, which help the model learn to generate music that aligns closely with detailed text prompts.
elements.sent-2411-493: While both AudioLDM and MusicLDM use LDMs and the CLAP framework, MusicLDM's enhancements make it particularly suited for generating music.
elements.sent-2411-494: The beat-synchronous data augmentation and retraining of the CLAP model on music-specific datasets allow MusicLDM to generate more musically coherent and varied outputs compared to the more general audio generation capabilities of AudioLDM.
elements.sent-2411-495: However, both models face limitations in precise control over the generated audio due to the global nature of CLAP embeddings and the gap between training (audio embeddings) and inference (text embeddings).
elements.sent-2411-496: AudioLDM 2~\citep{audioldm2 is an advanced text-to-music generation model that improves upon its predecessor, AudioLDM, by integrating more detailed text representations and refining the conditioning process to enhance the quality and controllability of the generated audio.}
elements.sent-2411-497: AudioLDM 2 generates high-fidelity audio, including music, from textual descriptions by fusing comprehensive text representations with advanced diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-498: This model is designed to address the limitations in earlier models regarding the precision and detail of the generated audio.
elements.sent-2411-499: The core components of AudioLDM 2 include the use of Contrastive Language-Audio Pretraining (CLAP) embeddings and a Latent Diffusion Model (LDM).
elements.sent-2411-500: The model operates through a two-step process: 1, Conditioning Information to LOA Translation: AudioLDM 2 introduces the concept of the ``Language of Audio" (LOA), an intermediate feature that bridges the gap between text and audio representations.
elements.sent-2411-501: The LOA is derived using a self-supervised pretrained AudioMAE~\citep{audiomae model, which captures both semantic and acoustic details of audio signals; 2, LOA to Audio Generation: The model uses the LOA to condition the latent diffusion model, which generates the final audio output.}
elements.sent-2411-502: By employing a cross-attention mechanism, AudioLDM 2 can incorporate detailed text representations directly into the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-503: This allows the model to utilise CFG, enhancing the precision and fidelity of the generated audio.
elements.sent-2411-504: AudioLDM 2 is trained on comprehensive datasets such as AudioSet and MusicCaps, which provide a diverse range of audio samples paired with textual descriptions.
