elements.sent-2411-505: This extensive training data enables the model to learn robust mappings between text and audio, improving its generalisation and performance across various audio generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-506: Stable Audio 1~\citep{sd1 is a diffusion model designed for generating long-form, variable-length stereo audio, including music, from text prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-507: Unlike earlier models that produce fixed-length outputs, Stable Audio 1 introduces timing conditioning to generate audio of varying lengths, making it suitable for creating long and complex audio sequences.
elements.sent-2411-508: Stable Audio 1 employs a 907M parameter U-Net, incorporating residual, self-attention, and cross-attention layers to denoise the latent representations conditioned on text and timing embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-509: Memory-efficient attention mechanisms enable the handling of longer audio sequences.
elements.sent-2411-510: Stable Audio 1 is trained on a dataset comprising over 800,000 audio files, totaling approximately 19,500 hours of music, sound effects, and single-instrument stems.
elements.sent-2411-511: The dataset, sourced from AudioSparx, includes detailed text metadata for effective text-to-audio generation.
elements.sent-2411-512: Stable Audio 2~\citep{sd2 is an advanced model designed for long-form music generation using latent diffusion techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-513: It builds upon the foundations of Stable Audio 1, enhancing its capabilities to generate longer, more complex music tracks with coherent structure.
elements.sent-2411-514: Stable Audio 2 can generate music up to 4 minutes and 45 seconds in length, a significant improvement over its predecessor.
elements.sent-2411-515: The core components of Stable Audio 2 include: 1.
elements.sent-2411-516: Diffusion-Transformer (DiT)~\citep{dit : Unlike the convolutional U-Net structure used in Stable Audio 1, Stable Audio 2 employs a Diffusion-Transformer (DiT).}
elements.sent-2411-517: The DiT integrates attention mechanisms and gated multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) with cross-attention layers to incorporate conditioning signals.
elements.sent-2411-518: This setup allows for efficient handling of long sequences and the integration of detailed text and timing information.
elements.sent-2411-519: The model uses rotary positional embeddings and efficient block-wise attention, combined with gradient checkpointing, to manage the computational and memory demands of processing long temporal contexts.
elements.sent-2411-520: The training process involves a multi-stage approach.
elements.sent-2411-521: Initially, the model is pretrained to generate music up to 3 minutes and 10 seconds long, followed by fine-tuning to extend the maximum length to 4 minutes and 45 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-522: This phased training ensures the model can handle extended sequences effectively.
elements.sent-2411-523: JEN-1~\citep{jen1 is a high-fidelity model designed for text-to-music generation, leveraging an omnidirectional diffusion model that integrates both autoregressive (AR) and non-autoregressive (NAR) training paradigms.}
elements.sent-2411-524: This dual approach allows JEN-1 to capture sequential dependencies in music (via AR training) while also benefiting from the efficiency of parallel generation (via NAR training).
elements.sent-2411-525: The core component of JEN-1 is its hybrid diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-526: This model operates within a noise-robust latent embedding space obtained from a masked audio autoencoder.
elements.sent-2411-527: The diffusion model utilises bidirectional modes to gather comprehensive context and unidirectional modes to capture sequential dependencies, providing a balanced approach to generating coherent and high-fidelity music.
elements.sent-2411-528: JEN-1 is trained on datasets such as MusicCaps, which contain extensive audio clips paired with textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-529: These datasets enable the model to learn the intricate mappings between text and music, enhancing its ability to generate music that aligns well with textual prompts.
elements.sent-2411-530: JEN-1 Composer~\citep{jen1composer extends the capabilities of the original JEN-1 model to facilitate high-fidelity multi-track music generation.}
elements.sent-2411-531: Building on JEN-1’s foundation, which integrates both autoregressive and non-autoregressive training for text-guided music generation, JEN-1 Composer introduces a unified framework that efficiently models the marginal, conditional, and joint distributions over multi-track music.
elements.sent-2411-532: JEN-1 Composer is designed for versatile and controllable multi-track music generation.
elements.sent-2411-533: This model extends the single-track architecture of JEN-1 by concatenating multi-track latent representations, thus enabling the explicit modelling of interdependencies between different tracks.
elements.sent-2411-534: The model employs a curriculum training strategy that incrementally transitions from single-track generation to multi-track generation, improving its capacity to generate coherent multi-track music.
elements.sent-2411-535: JEN-1 Composer is also trained on the same dataset.
elements.sent-2411-536: Additionally, the model leverages source separation tools like Spleeter and Demucs to augment its training data, allowing it to learn from a diverse range of musical compositions and enhancing its ability to generate high-fidelity music across multiple tracks.
elements.sent-2411-537: MusicFlow~\citep{musicflow explores a variant of diffusion models called flow matching for text-to-music generation.}
elements.sent-2411-538: This method aims to improve training stability and efficiency while generating high-quality music.
elements.sent-2411-539: Unlike diffusion models, flow matching focuses on modelling the conditional distribution of semantic and acoustic features using two flow matching networks.
elements.sent-2411-540: This approach achieves faster and more stable training with minor revisions to existing diffusion techniques.
elements.sent-2411-541: MusicFlow does not represent a significant breakthrough in text-to-music quality but opens a pathway for efficient model training.
elements.sent-2411-542: MusicFlow utilises a cascaded flow matching network for its generation process.
elements.sent-2411-543: This involves two networks: one for modelling the semantic features extracted from text descriptions and another for acoustic features.
elements.sent-2411-544: The training objective incorporates masked prediction, which enhances the model’s ability to generalise to tasks like music infilling and continuation.
elements.sent-2411-545: By employing a flow matching strategy, the model operates with fewer iterative steps and a smaller parameter size compared to conventional diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-546: MusicFlow is trained on an internal dataset comprising a large collection of music audio paired with textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-547: While specific datasets used for training are not publicly detailed, it is noted that MusicCaps was used for evaluation purposes, not training.
elements.sent-2411-548: This internal dataset enables the model to learn complex associations between text and music, allowing for efficient and effective music generation.
elements.sent-2411-549: Music language modelling plays a crucial role in text-to-music generation by enabling the system to understand and generate audio sequences.
elements.sent-2411-550: Several advanced models and techniques are employed to achieve this, including Vector Quantised Variational Autoencoders (VQ-VAEs), Residual Vector Quantisation (RVQ), and autoregressive models.
elements.sent-2411-551: Here, I will discuss these methods and their contributions to text-to-music generation.
elements.sent-2411-552: VQ-VAEs~\citep{vqvae are a type of autoencoder that incorporates discrete latent variables, making them particularly effective for modelling high-dimensional data such as audio.}
elements.sent-2411-553: In the context of music generation, VQ-VAEs encode the audio into a discrete latent space, which is then used to generate new musical sequences by generating new latent codes.
elements.sent-2411-554: In a VQ-VAE, the encoder \( E \) maps the input audio \( y \) to a discrete latent space \( z \): \begin{equation}\label{eq:encoder} z = E(y).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-555: The encoder transforms the high-dimensional audio input into a lower dimensional latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-556: This step is crucial for reducing the complexity of the data while preserving its essential characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-557: The decoder \( D \) then reconstructs the audio from the discrete latent variables: \begin{equation}\label{eq:decoder} \hat{y} = D(z).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-558: The decoder takes the compressed latent representation and reconstructs it back into the audio signal, attempting to reproduce the original input as closely as possible.
elements.sent-2411-559: The latent space is quantised to a finite set of possible values, which allows the model to capture the essential features of the audio: \begin{equation}z_q = \text{Quantize}(z).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-560: Quantisation involves mapping the continuous latent variables to a discrete set of values, making it easier for the model to learn and generate accurate representations of the audio data.
elements.sent-2411-561: RVQ~\citep{rvq is an extension of VQ-VAEs that improves the representation capacity by using multiple quantisation stages.}
elements.sent-2411-562: EnCodec is an advanced implementation of RVQ, developed by Meta AI, which leverages neural networks for high-fidelity audio compression and generation.
elements.sent-2411-563: In RVQ, the latent space is quantised in multiple stages, each refining the representation: \begin{equation}z_{q_1} = \text{Quantize}(z_1), \quad z_{q_2} = \text{Quantize}(z_2 | z_{q_1}), \quad \ldots .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-564: In the equation, \( z_{q_1 \) represents the quantised version of the latent variable \( z_1 \), obtained after the first quantisation stage.}
elements.sent-2411-565: This pattern continues for subsequent stages, where each quantised latent variable \( z_{q_i \) is refined by the previous stage’s quantised output, progressively improving the fidelity of the representation.}
elements.sent-2411-566: EnCodec~\citep{audiogen utilises a convolutional encoder-decoder architecture with residual vector quantisation.}
elements.sent-2411-567: It compresses the audio into a lower-dimensional representation and then reconstructs it, maintaining high fidelity.. Encoder: \begin{equation}\tag{\ref{eq:encoder}} z = E(y).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-568: The encoder consists of multiple convolutional layers followed by a residual vector quantisation bottleneck.. Decoder: \begin{equation}\tag{\ref{eq:decoder}} \hat{y} = D(z).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-569: The decoder mirrors the encoder, reconstructing the audio from the quantised latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-570: Performance: EnCodec is capable of compressing audio to various bitrates while preserving high audio quality.
elements.sent-2411-571: It supports both monophonic and stereophonic audio at different sample rates.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/audiolm.pdf} \caption{A simplified diagram of an audio language model. The encoder compresses audio waveforms into discrete tokens, and a auto-regressive model is responsible for generating new tokens. Text conditions can be fused through the cross-attention module.} \label{fig:audiolm}
elements.sent-2411-572: Autoregressive models are fundamental in generating sequences where each step depends on the previous ones, making them particularly well-suited for music generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-573: These models are designed to predict the next element in a sequence based on the previous elements, ensuring temporal coherence and consistency in the generated music.
elements.sent-2411-574: In the context of text-to-music generation, autoregressive models are often built on RVQ tokens.
elements.sent-2411-575: RVQ tokens are generated by compressing audio data into a lower-dimensional, quantised form using Residual Vector Quantisation.
elements.sent-2411-576: This hierarchical quantisation process allows for efficient and detailed audio representation, which is crucial for high-fidelity music generation.
elements.sent-2411-577: Residual Vector Quantisation involves multiple stages of quantisation.
elements.sent-2411-578: Each stage refines the previous stage's output, allowing for a detailed and accurate representation of the audio data.
elements.sent-2411-579: The quantised tokens generated through RVQ serve as the building blocks for the autoregressive models.. \begin{equation}z_{q_1} = \text{Quantize}(z_1), \quad z_{q_2} = \text{Quantize}(z_2 | z_{q_1}), \quad \ldots.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-580: Here, \( z_{q_i \) represents the quantised latent variables at each stage \( i \).}
elements.sent-2411-581: Autoregressive models in text-to-music generation typically employ transformer architectures due to their ability to handle long-range dependencies and sequential data efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-582: These models are conditioned on the quantised tokens generated by RVQ.
elements.sent-2411-583: Token Embedding: \begin{equation}\text{Token Embedding} = \text{Embed}(z_q)\end{equation} The quantised tokens \( z_q \) are embedded into a higher-dimensional space suitable for the transformer model.
elements.sent-2411-585: The autoregressive model generates the sequence one step at a time, with each step \( y_t \) conditioned on all previous steps \( y_{1:t-1 \).}
elements.sent-2411-586: Transformer Decoder: \begin{equation}\text{Decoder Output} = \text{TransformerDecoder}(\text{Token Embedding})\end{equation} The embedded tokens are passed through the transformer decoder, which predicts the next token in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-587: Autoregressive models for text-to-music generation are often conditioned on text embeddings to align the generated music with the given textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-588: This conditioning is achieved by incorporating text embeddings into the model's architecture.. \begin{equation}\text{Text Embedding} = \text{T5}(x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-589: Here, \( x \) represents the textual description, and T5 is a pretrained text encoder that generates text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-590: The model is trained end-to-end, optimising the likelihood of the observed sequences.
elements.sent-2411-591: During training, the model learns to predict the next token in the sequence based on the previous tokens and the text conditioning.. \begin{equation}L = -\sum_{t=1}^T \log p(y_t | y_{1:t-1}, x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-592: The training objective is to maximise the log-likelihood of the observed sequences, ensuring that the model generates sequences that are consistent with both the previous tokens and the text descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-593: Recent breakthroughs like MusicLM (2023) established the potential of language models for generating high-quality music, leveraging a hierarchical combination of SoundStream for audio synthesis and w2v-BERT for maintaining long-term coherence.
elements.sent-2411-594: The use of MuLan embeddings enabled the model to align music and text representations effectively, laying the groundwork for future advancements in generating coherent, stylistically rich music directly from textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-595: MusicGen (2023) continued this trend by integrating EnCodec and T5 into a single-stage transformer framework, focusing on efficient token processing to generate longer, high-fidelity music sequences.
elements.sent-2411-596: More recent models, such as MAGNeT (2024), introduced non-AR transformers to enhance generation speed without sacrificing quality.
elements.sent-2411-597: MAGNeT uses a masked generative approach, significantly reducing the time required to generate audio while still maintaining reasonable fidelity, thanks to its reliance on the EnCodec model for high-quality tokenised representations.
elements.sent-2411-598: StemGen (2024) took a different path by focusing on stem generation, offering greater flexibility in music production by generating individual instrumental tracks conditioned on existing stems and specific instrument categories.
elements.sent-2411-599: In parallel, symbolic-domain language models have made strides in generating music using discrete musical representations.
elements.sent-2411-600: MuseCoCo (2023) introduced a two-stage framework to separate text-to-attribute understanding and attribute-to-music generation, offering precise control over the musical attributes described in text.
elements.sent-2411-601: Models like ChatMusician (2024) extended these ideas by leveraging large language models (LLMs) and training them on text-based music representations such as ABC notation, further demonstrating the versatility of language models in symbolic music generation.
elements.sent-2411-602: Despite limitations in representing complex musical structures with character-level notation, these models have shown significant potential in treating music generation as a natural language task.
elements.sent-2411-603: MusicLM~\citep{musiclm is capable of generating music at 24 kHz that remains coherent over extended durations.}
elements.sent-2411-604: MusicLM generates raw audio, ensuring a natural and fluid progression in the music it creates.
elements.sent-2411-605: The core architecture of MusicLM utilises a combination of SoundStream~\citep{rvq for high-quality audio synthesis, w2v-BERT~\citep{w2vbert for maintaining long-term semantic coherence, and MuLan~\citep{mulan for joint music-text embeddings.}}}
elements.sent-2411-606: The SoundStream model provides acoustic tokens, while w2v-BERT offers semantic tokens, ensuring that both fine acoustic details and overall musical structure are captured.
elements.sent-2411-607: MuLan embeddings are used to condition the model, with music embeddings employed during training and text embeddings used during inference.
elements.sent-2411-608: This hierarchical approach allows MusicLM to balance the generation of detailed and coherent music with adherence to textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-609: MusicLM is trained on a large and diverse dataset consisting of 280,000 hours of music, enabling it to capture a wide range of musical styles and contexts.
elements.sent-2411-610: This extensive dataset allows the model to learn complex mappings between textual descriptions and musical outputs, ensuring high fidelity and stylistic accuracy in the generated music.
elements.sent-2411-611: MusicGen~\citep{musicgen integrates a single-stage transformer LM with efficient token interleaving patterns.}
elements.sent-2411-612: The transformer model processes sequences of tokens (discrete audio representations) efficiently, capturing long-range dependencies and enabling high-quality music generation from text inputs.
elements.sent-2411-613: The model is trained end-to-end with text embeddings incorporated into the neural networks that parameterise the diffusion and autoregressive processes.
elements.sent-2411-614: This allows the model to learn the complex relationships between text and music.
elements.sent-2411-615: MusicGen models are trained on 30-second chunks of audio but can generate longer sequences with a windowing approach.
elements.sent-2411-616: MAGNeT~\citep{magnet is designed to generate high-quality audio, including music, from textual descriptions using a single non-autoregressive transformer model.}
elements.sent-2411-617: Unlike autoregressive models that generate audio sequentially, MAGNeT predicts spans of masked tokens in parallel, allowing for faster audio generation.
elements.sent-2411-618: This parallel approach makes MAGNeT significantly faster than models like MusicGen, though it does not achieve the same level of audio quality.
elements.sent-2411-619: MaGNeT's approach is similar to VampNet~\citep{vampnet , while VampNet focuses more on unconditional music inpainting, and MaGNeT supports text conditioning.}
elements.sent-2411-620: The core component of MAGNeT is its non-autoregressive transformer architecture, which operates over multiple streams of audio tokens generated by EnCodec.
elements.sent-2411-621: This approach leverages the EnCodec model, which provides a high-quality latent representation of audio by using vector quantisation to encode the audio into discrete tokens.
elements.sent-2411-622: MAGNeT employs a unique masking strategy that focuses on spans of tokens rather than individual tokens, improving the efficiency and coherence of the generated audio.
elements.sent-2411-623: This approach contrasts with MusicGen, which uses an autoregressive transformer model, leading to differences in generation speed and quality.
elements.sent-2411-624: MAGNeT is trained on a substantial dataset of 16,000 hours of audio, sourced from licenced datasets such as the Meta Music Initiative Sound Collection, Shutterstock music collection, and Pond5 music collection.
elements.sent-2411-625: However, the exact datasets used for training are not publicly available due to legal agreements with the rights holders, and only a dummy dataset is provided for illustrative purposes.
elements.sent-2411-626: StemGen~\citep{stemgen is a music generation model that focuses on generating individual stems based on other stems, differing from other foundation models that typically aim to generate complete music tracks.}
elements.sent-2411-627: This approach allows for more granular control and flexibility in music production, making it suitable for applications where individual instrumental tracks need to be manipulated independently.
elements.sent-2411-628: StemGen employs a LLaMa-type transformer model, leveraging a context-mix and an instrument category as conditioning sources.
elements.sent-2411-629: This dual conditioning approach helps ensure that the generated stems align well with the existing context and specified instrumentation.
elements.sent-2411-630: The model operates on 32kHz audio, using EnCodec to encode the audio into discrete tokens, which are then processed by the transformer model.
elements.sent-2411-631: A trainable codebook with 18 entries, corresponding to the General MIDI instrument categories, is used to translate conditioning information into embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-632: StemGen is trained on a combination of the publicly available Slakh dataset, which consists of 145 hours of synthetic musical audio separated into stems, and an internal dataset of 500 hours of licenced human-played music, also separated into stems.
elements.sent-2411-633: The datasets include metadata specifying the instrument type of each stem, allowing the model to learn detailed mappings between the context-mix, instrument category, and the generated stems.
elements.sent-2411-634: This extensive training setup enables StemGen to handle a wide range of musical contexts and instrumentation accurately.
elements.sent-2411-635: As symbolic music generation models, the large-scale models below represent the latest methods for text-to-music generation.
elements.sent-2411-636: I list and discuss them as a comparison with the audio language modelling methods.
elements.sent-2411-637: MuseCoCo~\citep{musecoco is a model designed to generate symbolic music from text descriptions by employing a two-stage framework.}
elements.sent-2411-638: This approach separates the task into text-to-attribute understanding and attribute-to-music generation stages, each trained independently.
elements.sent-2411-639: This separation allows MuseCoCo to effectively interpret and control various musical attributes specified in the text, such as genre, mood, tempo, and key, enhancing the model's ability to generate music that aligns well with user specifications.
elements.sent-2411-640: The core components of MuseCoCo include a robust attribute extraction mechanism and a music generation model.
elements.sent-2411-641: The first stage, text-to-attribute understanding, uses a pretrained language model to extract diverse musical attributes from textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-642: These attributes are then transformed into prefix tokens, which guide the subsequent music generation process.
elements.sent-2411-643: In the second stage, attribute-to-music generation, the model leverages these tokens to produce symbolic music.
elements.sent-2411-644: This two-stage approach allows the model to handle large amounts of unlabeled data efficiently, using self-supervised methods to extract musical attributes and combining them to achieve complex musical expressions.
elements.sent-2411-645: MuseCoCo is trained on extensive symbolic music datasets, allowing it to learn a wide variety of musical styles and structures.
elements.sent-2411-646: The training data consists of a large collection of symbolic music notations paired with textual descriptions, which include both objective attributes like tempo and key, and subjective attributes like mood and genre.
elements.sent-2411-647: This dataset is primarily constructed using publicly available symbolic music data, combined with synthesised text-attribute pairs to enrich the training set.
elements.sent-2411-648: This study investigates the use of pretrained language models (BERT, GPT-2, and BART) for the task of text-to-music generation.
elements.sent-2411-649: It aims to generate complete and semantically consistent symbolic music scores directly from natural language descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-650: By leveraging pretrained checkpoints, the study explores how these models can be adapted for generating music notation from text, assessing their performance and the benefits they bring to the text-to-music generation process.
elements.sent-2411-651: The core component of the study is the use of pretrained language models to generate music in ABC notation.
elements.sent-2411-652: This choice of notation, however, presents certain limitations due to its character-level tokenisation, which can struggle with capturing more complex musical nuances and dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-653: The models are fine-tuned on a dataset of 282,870 English text-music pairs, using publicly available checkpoints for BERT, GPT-2, and BART.
elements.sent-2411-654: The training involves initialising the model parameters from these checkpoints and adapting them for music generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-655: Despite these efforts, the study acknowledges the inherent weaknesses of ABC notation in accurately representing the intricate details of music, which can limit the expressiveness and quality of the generated music.
elements.sent-2411-656: The training data for this study is derived from a combination of public datasets, but the specific dataset used for training the models has not been publicly released due to copyright restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-657: Instead, the authors have made the WikiMusicText (WikiMT) dataset available, which includes 1,010 pairs of text-music data for evaluation purposes.
elements.sent-2411-658: This dataset helps in evaluating the performance of the models and provides a benchmark for future research.
elements.sent-2411-659: The study highlights the potential of pretrained language models in the music generation domain while also pointing out the challenges and limitations posed by the choice of music notation and the availability of high-quality training data.
elements.sent-2411-660: ChatMusician~\citep{chatmusician is a novel large language model (LLM) developed to understand and generate music using a text-compatible music representation known as ABC notation.}
elements.sent-2411-661: This model integrates musical abilities intrinsically within the LLM framework, allowing it to process and generate music through pure text tokenisation without relying on external multi-modal structures.
elements.sent-2411-662: The primary aim of ChatMusician is to demonstrate that LLMs can be adapted to handle the language of music, treating it as a second language and enabling the generation of structured, full-length musical compositions conditioned on various musical attributes like chords, melodies, motifs, and forms.
elements.sent-2411-663: The core components of ChatMusician include continual pre-training and fine-tuning of the LLaMA2 model on a curated dataset called MusicPile.
elements.sent-2411-664: MusicPile consists of 4.16 billion tokens, encompassing a variety of music scores, music-related knowledge, and general language data.
elements.sent-2411-665: The dataset includes public datasets such as Pile, Falcon-RefinedWeb, and Wikipedia, alongside specialised music data like KernScores and Bach music scores.
elements.sent-2411-666: Additionally, synthetic data generated with the help of GPT-4 supplements the training corpus, enhancing the model's musical understanding and generation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-667: Despite its innovative approach, ChatMusician's reliance on ABC notation presents limitations.
elements.sent-2411-668: ABC notation, a character-level music representation, can struggle to capture complex musical nuances and dependencies, potentially limiting the expressiveness and detail of the generated music.
elements.sent-2411-669: However, the model demonstrates significant potential by integrating musical capabilities into LLMs, showing that these models can serve as effective tools for music composition and understanding, even if they do not achieve state-of-the-art performance in symbolic and audio music generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-670: ChatMusician offers a novel approach in the landscape of text-to-music generation by leveraging the capabilities of LLMs to process and generate music through text alone.
elements.subsection-2411-011: Comparison Between Two Types of Backbone Models
elements.sent-2411-671: In the field of text-to-music generation, two primary model types have emerged: diffusion models and audio language models, each with unique advantages and challenges.
elements.sent-2411-672: Diffusion models, such as those used in Moûsai, Noise2Music, TANGO, Mustango, AudioLDM, and MusicFlow, focus on iteratively refining an initial noisy signal to generate high-fidelity audio outputs.
elements.sent-2411-673: They excel in capturing fine-grained audio details and producing high-quality, coherent music.
elements.sent-2411-674: Advanced sampling methods like DDIM and DPM-Solver have significantly reduced the time required for generating samples, making diffusion models more efficient.
elements.sent-2411-675: Furthermore, techniques like progressive distillation can convert diffusion models to more efficient generative adversarial networks (GANs), balancing quality and speed.
elements.sent-2411-676: These models are well-suited for applications where audio fidelity and nuanced musical detail are paramount, despite requiring significant computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-677: Audio language models like MusicGen and MusicLM leverage pretrained language models to understand and generate music.
elements.sent-2411-678: These models often use hierarchical structures to balance long-term coherence with detailed audio generation.
elements.sent-2411-679: Techniques such as non-autoregressive sampling, as seen in MAGNeT, have further enhanced the efficiency of these models, making them faster and more suitable for real-time applications.
elements.sent-2411-680: These models are generally more efficient than diffusion models, providing reasonable audio quality with faster generation times.
elements.sent-2411-681: They are ideal for interactive music creation and scenarios requiring quick iterations.
elements.sent-2411-682: The choice between these two types depends on the specific application requirements.
elements.sent-2411-683: Diffusion models are preferable for tasks that demand high-fidelity audio and detailed musical nuances, especially in offline settings where generation quality is more important than speed.
elements.sent-2411-684: On the other hand, audio language models are better suited for real-time applications and interactive scenarios due to their efficiency and quicker generation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-685: The integration of fast sampling methods and efficient tokenisation techniques in both types of models continues to push the boundaries, making it crucial to consider the specific needs and constraints of the application when choosing the appropriate model type.
elements.sent-2411-686: Fine-tuning pretrained models is a critical process in adapting powerful machine learning systems to specific tasks, such as music generation.
elements.sent-2411-687: This process allows the models to leverage their general understanding while acquiring specialised knowledge tailored to the task at hand.
elements.sent-2411-688: Parameter-Efficient Fine-tuning (PEFT) is a subset of this process that seeks to achieve this adaptation with minimal increase in the number of parameters, thus maintaining computational efficiency and reducing the risk of overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-689: One of the key challenges in music generation is capturing the complex and nuanced structures inherent in musical compositions.
elements.sent-2411-690: Traditional fine-tuning methods can be computationally expensive and may require a large number of additional parameters.
elements.sent-2411-691: PEFT techniques, such as LoRA and Llama-adapter, offer innovative solutions to this problem by introducing small, yet effective, modifications to the model's existing architecture.
elements.sent-2411-692: LoRA, for instance, employs low-rank matrices to adapt the attention mechanisms of transformers.
elements.sent-2411-693: By fine-tuning the cross-attention and self-attention layers, LoRA allows the model to better understand and generate music with a nuanced grasp of its structure.
elements.sent-2411-694: The Llama-adapter, although a conceptual technique, presents an intriguing approach to adapting the latent space of models like LLaMA, which are known for their efficiency in language tasks.
elements.sent-2411-695: By transforming the latent vectors with a matrix that represents musical characteristics, the Llama-adapter could potentially enhance the model's ability to generate music that aligns with specific stylistic or structural requirements.
elements.sent-2411-696: LoRA~\citep{lora is a technique designed to fine-tune pretrained models with minimal additional parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-697: In the context of music generation, LoRA can be particularly effective for adapting the model's cross-attention and self-attention mechanisms to better capture the musical structure.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{figs/lora.pdf} \caption{Diagram of the Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) algorithm. LoRA is a PEFT method that decomposes a large matrix into two smaller low-rank matrices in the attention layers, which facilitates model finetuning.} \label{fig:lora}
elements.sent-2411-698: Cross-Attention in Transformers: The cross-attention mechanism in transformers allows the model to focus on different parts of the input sequence when generating each token.
elements.sent-2411-699: By applying LoRA to the cross-attention layers, we can introduce low-rank matrices \( A \) that adapt the attention weights \( w \) as follows: \begin{equation}w' = A \cdot w,\end{equation} where \( A \) is a low-rank matrix that captures the fine-grained control needed for music generation.
elements.sent-2411-700: Self-Attention in Transformers: Self-attention allows the model to focus on different parts of the generated sequence itself.
elements.sent-2411-701: Fine-tuning this with LoRA involves adapting the self-attention weights \( v \) and \( k \) using a low-rank matrix \( B \) as follows: \begin{equation}v' = B \cdot v, \quad k' = B \cdot k\end{equation} This adaptation enables the model to better represent the internal structure of the music.
elements.sent-2411-702: Diffusion Cross-Attention: In models that combine diffusion processes with attention mechanisms, LoRA can be applied to the cross-attention layers to control the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-703: The diffusion cross-attention can be formulated as: \begin{equation}w' = D \cdot A \cdot w,\end{equation} where \( D \) represents the diffusion process matrix and \( A \) is the low-rank adaptation matrix.
elements.subsection-2411-013: Llama-Adapter: Adaptation for Latent Space Control
elements.sent-2411-704: Adapting Latent Space: The Llama-adapter could be used to adapt the latent space of the LLaMA model to better represent musical features.
elements.sent-2411-705: This could involve modifying the latent vectors \( z \) with a transformation matrix \( T \) as follows: \begin{equation}z' = T \cdot z,\end{equation} where \( T \) is a matrix that captures the musical characteristics to be emphasised.
elements.sent-2411-706: Application in Music Generation: The adapted latent space could then be used to influence the generation process, ensuring that the generated music adheres to specific stylistic or structural requirements.
elements.sent-2411-707: This could be particularly useful for generating music in a specific genre or style.
elements.sent-2411-708: Fine-Tuning with Music Data: After adapting the latent space, the model could be further fine-tuned on a dataset of music to refine its understanding of musical patterns and structures.
elements.sent-2411-709: This would involve optimising the parameters of the Llama-adapter to minimise the difference between the generated music and the target music.
elements.section-2411-010: Controllability and Editability of Pretrained Models
elements.sent-2411-710: The focus of this section is on how to enable and extend the controllability and editability of pretrained music generation models, making them more aligned with real-world music-making applications, such as music inpainting, conditional music generation, music variation generation, etc. This involves exploring various approaches and techniques used in the field, including integrating control mechanisms during model training, enhancing control post-training, and employing sophisticated editing methods.
elements.subsection-2411-014: Approaches to Enhancing Controllability and Editability
elements.sent-2411-711: Previous approaches to control in small generative models often rely on disentangling representations in latent space~\citep{luo2019learning, polydis, xai .}
elements.sent-2411-712: For large-scale music generative models, control mechanisms are typically implemented through various strategies:.
elements.sent-2411-713: Integrating Trainable Control Modules into Pretrained Models: This category focuses on augmenting pretrained large-scale models with additional control mechanisms, enabling the generation of music that adheres to specific parameters without retraining the entire model.
elements.sent-2411-714: Examples include Music ControlNet~\citep{musiccontrolnet , Diff-A-Riff~\citep{diffariff , and JASCO~\citep{jasco , which introduce control modules or conditioning methods to adjust dynamics, melody, rhythm, and other musical attributes during the generation process.}}}
elements.sent-2411-715: Specialised Editing Models: These models are designed explicitly for editing existing music content based on user instructions, often utilising text guidance to modify or enhance music.
elements.sent-2411-716: For instance, AUDIT~\citep{audit and InstructME~\citep{instructme perform various audio editing tasks such as adding, dropping, replacing, inpainting, and super-resolution, guided by human text instructions.}}
elements.sent-2411-717: They provide fine-grained control over the editing process, allowing users to adjust specific aspects of the music while preserving others.
elements.sent-2411-718: Agent-Based Methods for Compositional Music Generation and Editing: This approach employs agent-based systems or multi-modal AI frameworks to enable interactive and compositional music generation and editing, integrating large language models with other modalities.
elements.sent-2411-719: Examples include AudioGPT~\citep{audiogpt , which incorporates audio foundation models to enhance large language models' capabilities in processing complex audio information and engaging in spoken dialogues, and M$^2$UGen~\citep{m2ugen , which leverages large language models for multi-modal music understanding and generation from diverse sources such as music, images, and videos.}}
elements.sent-2411-720: Inference-Time Optimisation Methods: These methods focus on controlling and editing music generation during the inference stage of pretrained models without requiring additional training or fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-721: Techniques like DITTO~\citep{ditto , SMITIN~\citep{smitin , ZETA~\citep{zeta , and the approach by \citet{simon2024 utilise inference-time optimisation, classifier probes, or zero-shot editing to steer the output of generative models toward desired musical characteristics.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-722: This allows for dynamic adjustments and fine-tuning of the generated music to better match user intentions.
elements.subsection-2411-015: Integrating Trainable Control Modules into Pretrained Models
elements.sent-2411-723: Music ControlNet~\citep{musiccontrolnet presents a novel approach to enhancing the controllability of music generation models.}
elements.sent-2411-724: It is pioneering work that adapts the ControlNet architecture, commonly used in image generation, for the domain of music.
elements.sent-2411-725: The primary goal is to introduce dynamic, melody, and rhythm controls that allow users to fine-tune the output of music generation models more precisely.
elements.sent-2411-726: By doing so, Music ControlNet aims to bridge the gap between automated music generation and real-world music production requirements.
elements.sent-2411-727: Music ControlNet integrates control signals into the generative process of large-scale transformer-based models.
elements.sent-2411-728: The input consists of control signals and initial music input.
elements.sent-2411-729: These control signals, which include dynamic markings, melody lines, and rhythmic patterns, are processed through a series of transformations and then incorporated into the self-attention and cross-attention layers of the transformer.
elements.sent-2411-730: This integration allows the model to adjust various aspects of the music generation process based on the provided control signals, ensuring that the generated music adheres to user specifications.
elements.sent-2411-731: The model architecture can be formally described as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-732: Input Representation: The input consists of control signals \( c \) and initial music input \( x \).
elements.sent-2411-733: Control signals can include dynamic markings, melody lines, or rhythmic patterns.
elements.sent-2411-734: Control Module Integration: The control module is integrated into the transformer-based generative model.
elements.sent-2411-735: The control signals \( c \) are processed through a series of transformations: \begin{equation}c' = W_c \cdot c + b_c,\end{equation} where \( W_c \) and \( b_c \) are weight and bias parameters for the control signals.
elements.sent-2411-736: Attention Mechanisms: The modified control signals \( c' \) are incorporated into the self-attention and cross-attention layers of the transformer.
elements.sent-2411-737: For self-attention, the attention weights \( w \) are adjusted based on \( c' \): \begin{equation}w' = A \cdot (w + c'),\end{equation} where \( A \) is a low-rank adaptation matrix.
elements.sent-2411-738: Similarly, for cross-attention, the process is: \begin{equation}w'' = B \cdot (w' + c').\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-739: Output Generation: The adjusted attention weights are then used to generate the output music sequence \( y \): \begin{equation}y = \text{Decoder}(w'').\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-740: This process ensures that the generated music adheres to the control signals provided by the user.
elements.sent-2411-741: Music ControlNet is evaluated on a diverse dataset comprising various music genres and styles, including both MIDI files and audio recordings annotated with dynamic, melody, and rhythm information.
elements.sent-2411-742: The experiments focus on measuring the controllability and quality of the generated music.
elements.sent-2411-743: Results indicate significant improvements in controllability compared to baseline models, allowing users to effectively adjust dynamics, melody, and rhythm.
elements.sent-2411-744: The quality of the generated music, assessed through both objective metrics like pitch accuracy and rhythmic consistency and subjective human evaluations, demonstrates that Music ControlNet produces high-quality music that closely aligns with user specifications.
elements.sent-2411-745: The model is based on Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs), offering control through audio references, text prompts, or a combination of both, while producing 48kHz stereo audio.
elements.sent-2411-746: At the heart of the Diff-A-Riff system are two critical technological elements: a Consistency Autoencoder (CAE) with a high compression rate, which improves inference time and memory usage, and the expressive power of Elucidated Diffusion Models (EDMs).
elements.sent-2411-747: EDMs are known for their robust handling of complex data distributions and enhanced efficiency in model parameterisation and inference.
elements.sent-2411-748: The model is trained to reconstruct accompaniments given a context and a CLAP (Cross-modal Latent Audio Processing) embedding derived from a random sub-segment of the target accompaniment itself.
elements.sent-2411-749: JASCO is a temporally controlled text-to-music generation model that utilises both symbolic and audio-based conditions.
elements.sent-2411-750: This approach allows for the generation of high-quality music samples that are conditioned on global text descriptions as well as fine-grained local controls.
elements.sent-2411-751: The model is based on the Flow Matching modelling paradigm combined with a novel conditioning method, enabling local and global control over music generation.
elements.sent-2411-752: The components of JASCO include a source separation network for drum extraction, an F0 saliency detector model for melody extraction, and a chord progression extraction model for harmonic conditioning.
elements.sent-2411-753: The model leverages a continuous latent representation of audio, obtained from a compression model, and incorporates discrete token sequences for audio conditioning.
elements.sent-2411-754: JASCO applies information bottleneck layers in conjunction with temporal blurring to extract relevant information with respect to specific controls.
elements.sent-2411-755: This allows the incorporation of both symbolic and audio-based conditions in the same text-to-music model.
elements.sent-2411-756: In terms of algorithmic formulas, the paper introduces the Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) objective function, which is defined as: \begin{equation}L_\text{CFM}(\theta; z_0, z_1, t|Y) = | v_\theta(z, t|Y) - (z_1 - (1 - \sigma_{min}) \cdot z_0) |^2.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-048: \begin{equation}L_CFM(\theta; z_0, z_1, t|Y) = | v_\theta(z, t|Y) - (z_1 - (1 - \sigma_{min}) \cdot z_0) |^2.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-757: The model is trained to predict the vector field of the continuous latent audio variable \( z \), given \( t \) and a set of conditions \( Y \).
elements.sent-2411-758: Additionally, the paper discusses the use of a weighted loss function during training, which is given by: \begin{equation}L_\text{WeightedCFM} = \mathbb{E}_{t \sim U(0,1), z_0 \sim N(0,1), z_1 \sim S} \left[ (1 + t) \cdot L_{CFM}(\theta; z_0, z_1, t|Y) \right].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-049: \begin{equation}L_WeightedCFM = E_{t \sim U(0,1), z_0 \sim N(0,1), z_1 \sim S} \left[ (1 + t) \cdot L_{CFM}(\theta; z_0, z_1, t|Y) \right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-759: This model is designed to perform various audio editing tasks such as adding, dropping, replacement, inpainting, and super-resolution, guided by human text instructions.
elements.sent-2411-760: Unlike previous methods that rely on pretrained text-to-audio models and require complete descriptions of the output audio, AUDIT utilises simple edit instructions, enhancing its flexibility and suitability for real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-761: The model demonstrates a novel approach to integrating control mechanisms within audio editing tasks, focusing on the editability and controllability of the audio output based on textual guidance.
elements.sent-2411-762: This work pioneers the field of specialised text-guided audio editing models, providing a foundation for subsequent research in this domain.
elements.sent-2411-763: AUDIT employs a control mechanism that leverages triplet training data consisting of instructions, input audio, and output audio.
elements.sent-2411-764: The model is trained in a supervised manner, utilising the input audio and text instructions as conditions to generate the edited audio output.
elements.sent-2411-765: This approach allows the model to automatically learn which segments of the audio need modification, thereby enhancing control over the editing process.
elements.sent-2411-766: The control mechanism in AUDIT is distinct as it does not require a full description of the target audio, aligning with the practical scenarios where detailed descriptions may not be available.
elements.sent-2411-767: This method of control is innovative as it directly uses the input audio as conditional input, forcing the model to ensure consistency of unedited segments before and after the editing process.
elements.sent-2411-768: AUDIT comprises several components, including a variational autoencoder (VAE), a T5 text encoder, a diffusion network, and a vocoder.
elements.sent-2411-769: The VAE model projects the input mel-spectrogram into a latent space and reconstructs it, while the T5 encoder converts text instructions into embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-770: The diffusion network operates in the latent space, guided by the text embeddings and the latent representation of the input audio, to generate the edited audio.
elements.sent-2411-771: The vocoder then reconstructs the waveform from the output mel-spectrogram.
elements.sent-2411-772: The training loss of the autoencoder is expressed as: \begin{equation}L_\text{VAE} = \lambda_1 L_1 + \lambda_2 L_2 + \lambda_\text{KL} L_\text{KL} + \lambda_\text{GAN} L_\text{GAN},\end{equation} where \( L_1 \) and \( L_2 \) are the L1 and L2 reconstruction losses, \( L_\text{KL \) is the Kullback-Leibler loss, and \( L_\text{GAN \) is the GAN loss.}}
elements.sent-2411-773: The latent diffusion model is trained to learn the distribution \( p(z_\text{out | z_\text{in , c_\text{text ) \), where \( z_\text{out \) is the latent representation of the edited mel-spectrogram, \( z_\text{in \) is the latent representation of the input mel-spectrogram, and \( c_\text{text \) is the embedding of the editing instruction.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-774: The training loss for the latent diffusion model is given by: \begin{equation}L_{LDM} = \mathbb{E}_{z_\text{in}, z_\text{out}, \text{instruction}} \left[ \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)} \left[ || \epsilon_{\theta}(z_t, t, z_\text{in}, c_\text{text}) - \epsilon ||^2 \right] \right].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-051: \begin{equation}L_{LDM} = E_{z_in, z_out, instruction} \left[ E_{\epsilon \sim N(0, I)} \left[ || \epsilon_{\theta}(z_t, t, z_in, c_text) - \epsilon ||^2 \right] \right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-775: The model is trained and evaluated on several datasets, including AudioCaps, AudioSet, FSD50K, and ESC50.
elements.sent-2411-776: These datasets consist of audio clips with corresponding labels or captions, providing a rich source of data for training the triplet data required for the various editing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-777: The training process involves generating approximately 0.6 million triplet data points, covering the five editing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-778: InstructME leverages natural language instructions to perform music editing tasks such as adding, removing, extracting, replacing, and remixing instrument tracks.
elements.sent-2411-779: The framework is designed to maintain the intrinsic harmony and coherence of music, which is often compromised by direct applications of image and audio modification techniques.
elements.sent-2411-780: InstructME employs a multi-scale aggregation strategy and incorporates chord progression matrices to enhance harmonic consistency during editing.
elements.sent-2411-781: Additionally, it utilises a chunk transformer to handle extended musical pieces by discerning long-term temporal dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-782: The framework's effectiveness is demonstrated through both subjective and objective evaluations, outperforming previous systems in music quality, text relevance, and harmony.
elements.sent-2411-783: InstructME is composed of several key components, including a variational autoencoder (VAE), a T5 text encoder, a diffusion network, and a chunk transformer.
elements.sent-2411-784: The VAE model compresses the input audio into a latent representation, which is then reconstructed by the decoder.
elements.sent-2411-785: The T5 encoder converts text instructions into embeddings that guide the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-786: The diffusion model, based on a time-conditional U-Net, generates new audio samples from noisy embeddings, conditioned on text and source music embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-787: The chunk transformer addresses the computational inefficiency of self-attention in long music sequences by processing the data in chunks, thereby modelling long-term dependencies with reduced computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-788: The control mechanism in InstructME is achieved through a combination of classifier-free guidance and classifier guidance, allowing for both fine-grained semantic control and flexibility in generation.
elements.sent-2411-789: The objective function for model optimisation is the reweighted bound: \begin{equation}L_\text{DM} = \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon, t, z_0} \left[ | \epsilon - \epsilon_{\theta}(t, T(y), z_s, z_t) |_2^2 \right].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-052: \begin{equation}L_DM = E_{\epsilon, t, z_0} \left[ | \epsilon - \epsilon_{\theta}(t, T(y), z_s, z_t) |_2^2 \right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-790: In addition, the framework incorporates multi-scale aggregation and chord conditioning to improve consistency and harmony: \begin{equation}L_\text{CDM} = \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon, t, z_0} \left[ | \epsilon - \epsilon_{\theta}(t, p_s, z_s, z_t) |_2^2 \right],\end{equation} where \(p_s\) denotes the chord progression matrix of the source music.
elements.formula-2411-053: \begin{equation}L_CDM = E_{\epsilon, t, z_0} \left[ | \epsilon - \epsilon_{\theta}(t, p_s, z_s, z_t) |_2^2 \right],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-791: InstructME is trained on a collection of 417 hours of music audio, resampled to a 24kHz sample rate and divided into non-overlapping 10-second clips.
elements.sent-2411-792: The training data includes triplets of text instructions, source music, and target music, covering remixing, adding, and replacement tasks.
elements.subsection-2411-017: Agent-based Methods for Compositional Music Generation and Editing
elements.sent-2411-793: Unlike regular LLMs that primarily focus on text, AudioGPT incorporates audio foundation models to enable understanding and generation tasks across speech, music, sound, and talking head modalities.
elements.sent-2411-794: The system aims to improve the controllability and editability of pretrained text-to-music models by integrating these audio capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-795: The control mechanisms employed by AudioGPT include the use of speech recognition, text-to-speech, and audio-to-text models to facilitate dialogue and audio processing, thereby enhancing the system's ability to interact with users in a more natural and intuitive manner.
elements.sent-2411-796: AudioGPT is composed of several components that work in tandem to achieve its objectives.
elements.sent-2411-797: The model can be divided into four main stages: 1.
elements.sent-2411-798: Modality Transformation: This stage uses input/output interfaces such as automatic speech recognition (ASR) and text-to-speech (TTS) to transform between speech and text, bridging the gap between spoken language models and text-based LLMs.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-799: Task Analysis: The dialogue engine and prompt manager in this stage help the LLM understand the user's intention to process audio information.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-800: Model Assignment: Here, the LLM assigns audio foundation models based on structured arguments for prosody, timbre, and language control.. 4.
elements.sent-2411-801: Response Generation: After the execution of foundation models, responses are generated and returned to the users.
elements.sent-2411-802: These components collectively enable AudioGPT to perform a wide range of tasks, including generating audio from text, synthesising speech and singing voices, and processing audio for various applications.
elements.sent-2411-803: The authors aim to bridge the gap in research that combines both understanding and generation using LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-804: The M$^2$UGen framework integrates the capabilities of LLMs to comprehend and generate music from diverse sources of inspiration, including music, images, and videos.
elements.sent-2411-805: This is achieved through the use of pretrained models such as MERT, ViT, ViViT, and LLaMA 2, and music generation models like AudioLDM 2 and MusicGen.
elements.sent-2411-806: The framework also employs the MULLaMA model to generate extensive datasets that support text/image/video-to-music generation, facilitating the training of the M$^2$UGen framework.
elements.sent-2411-807: The M$^2$UGen framework employs a systematic approach to controllability and editability in text-to-music models.
elements.sent-2411-808: It incorporates multiple modal encoders to represent image, video, and music inputs, utilising ViT and ViViT for image and video modalities, and MERT as the music encoder.
elements.sent-2411-809: The feature representations from these encoders are fed into understanding adaptors, which are then comprehended by the LLaMA 2 model.
elements.sent-2411-810: For music generation, the framework explores two music decoders, AudioLDM 2 and MusicGen.
elements.sent-2411-811: The control mechanism in M2UGen is achieved through the integration of these components, allowing for the generation of music based on multi-modal inputs.
elements.sent-2411-812: The framework also supports music editing based on natural language prompts, enhancing its editability.
elements.sent-2411-813: The M2UGen framework is evaluated on a range of tasks, including music question answering, text-to-music generation, image-to-music generation, video-to-music generation, and music editing.
elements.sent-2411-814: The authors have generated four datasets to train the model: MUCaps, MUImage, MUVideo, and MUEdit.
elements.sent-2411-815: These datasets consist of text-music pairs, image-music pairs, video-music pairs, and music editing instructions, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-816: The model is trained in a phased manner, initially focusing on comprehending diverse modalities, followed by refining the LLaMA 2 model's capability to condition music generation based on input captions.
elements.sent-2411-817: The final training stage employs a LoRA training strategy to fine-tune the LLaMA 2 model, multi-modal understanding adapters, and output projection layer.
elements.sent-2411-818: Unlike methods that focus on integrated control during pre-training or injecting modules like adapters for control, DITTO operates by optimising the initial noise latents to achieve a target output.
elements.sent-2411-819: This approach allows for fine-grained control over various musical features without the need for further training or fine-tuning of the underlying model.
elements.sent-2411-820: The control mechanism in DITTO is based on optimising an arbitrary differentiable feature matching loss.
elements.sent-2411-821: This enables the model to adjust its output to match a desired feature, such as intensity, melody, or musical structure.
elements.sent-2411-822: The framework leverages gradient checkpointing to ensure memory efficiency during the optimisation process.
elements.sent-2411-823: This method stands out as it provides a training-free way to control pretrained models, offering flexibility and efficiency in music generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-824: The core components of the DITTO model include the pretrained diffusion model, a feature extractor function \(f(\cdot)\), a loss function \(L\), and an optimiser.
elements.sent-2411-825: The algorithm operates by initialising the noise latents \(x_T\), running the diffusion sampling process to generate a music spectrogram \(x_0\), extracting features from the generated content, and then optimising the initial noise latents to fit any differentiable loss.
elements.sent-2411-826: The pseudo-code for the DITTO algorithm is as follows:.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Pseudocode} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: $\epsilon_\theta$, Sampler, $T$, $f$, $L$, $y$, $x_T$, $c$, $K$, $g$ \For{$i = 1$ to $K$} \State $x_t \leftarrow x_T$ \For{$t = T$ to $1$} \State $x_{t-1} = \text{Checkpoint}(\text{Sampler}, \epsilon_\theta, x_t, t, c)$ \EndFor \State $\hat{y} = f(x_0)$ \State $x_T \leftarrow x_T - g(\nabla_{x_T} L(\hat{y}, y))$ \EndFor \State Output: $x_0$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-827: In this algorithm, \(\epsilon_\theta\) represents the diffusion model, \(T\) is the number of sampling steps, \(f\) is the feature extractor, \(L\) is the loss function, \(y\) is the target feature, \(x_T\) is the initial noise latent, \(c\) is the text conditioning, \(K\) is the number of optimisation steps, and \(g\) is the optimiser.
elements.sent-2411-828: Unlike previous methods that focus on integrated control during pre-training or injecting modules for control, SMITIN employs classifier probes to steer the model's attention heads towards capturing specific musical traits.
elements.sent-2411-829: These probes are trained on a small dataset of audio examples and are used to dynamically adjust the intervention strength at each generation step.
elements.sent-2411-830: This ensures that the output incorporates the desired musical characteristics while maintaining temporal coherence.
elements.sent-2411-831: The method is validated for both audio continuation and text-to-music applications, demonstrating its effectiveness in adding controls to large generative models without the need for retraining or fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-832: The core components of the SMITIN model are the pretrained autoregressive music transformer, classifier probes, and a self-monitoring intervention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-833: The transformer generates music by predicting a sequence of audio frames, with each layer consisting of multiple attention heads.
elements.sent-2411-834: The classifier probes are trained to recognise specific musical traits and are used to modify the attention heads' outputs.
elements.sent-2411-835: The self-monitoring intervention mechanism dynamically adjusts the intervention strength based on the probe output, preventing excessive intervention that could disrupt the music's temporal coherence.
elements.sent-2411-836: The model also incorporates a soft-weighting approach to determine the weights of the attention heads, eliminating the need for empirical tuning.
elements.sent-2411-837: The first technique, named ZEro-shot Text-based Audio (ZETA) editing, adapts a method from the image domain, allowing for text-guided manipulations of audio signals.
elements.sent-2411-838: The second technique, called ZEro-shot UnSupervised (ZEUS) editing, introduces an unsupervised approach to discover semantically meaningful editing directions.
elements.sent-2411-839: These methods aim to enhance the controllability and editability of text-to-music models without the need for training or test-time optimisation, thereby providing a more flexible and efficient means of audio editing.
elements.sent-2411-840: The control mechanism employed in this paper is primarily through text guidance in the ZETA technique and semantic perturbation in the ZEUS technique.
elements.sent-2411-841: In ZETA, the authors utilise classifier-free guidance (CFG) to steer the generative process towards the desired text prompt, enabling edits such as changing the style or genre of a musical piece.
elements.sent-2411-842: In contrast, ZEUS relies on perturbing the output of the denoiser in the directions of the top principal components (PCs) of the posterior.
elements.sent-2411-843: This unsupervised approach allows for the generation of variations that adhere to the original key, rhythm, and style, but are not limited by text descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-844: The components of the model include the pretrained AudioLDM2 model, which operates in a latent space and generates mel-spectograms conditioned on text.
elements.sent-2411-845: These mel-spectograms are then decoded into waveforms using HiFi-GAN.
elements.sent-2411-846: The DDPM inversion is used to extract latent noise vectors corresponding to the source signal.
elements.sent-2411-847: For the ZETA technique, the generative process is controlled by changing the text prompt supplied to the denoiser model.
elements.sent-2411-848: For the ZEUS technique, the output of the denoiser is perturbed along the top PCs of the posterior covariance.
elements.sent-2411-849: The algorithms presented in the paper involve the DDPM inversion process and the application of PCs for editing.
elements.sent-2411-850: For the ZEUS technique, the perturbation of the denoiser's output is given by: \begin{equation}x_{t-1} = \mu_t(x_t) + \gamma c_t \lambda_i^{1/2} v_{i|t'} + \sigma_t z_t, \quad t = T, \ldots, 1\end{equation} where \( c_t \) is a correction factor, \( \lambda_i^{1/2 \) and \( v_{i|t' \) are the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the posterior covariance, and \( \gamma \) is a user-chosen parameter controlling the strength of the modification.}}
elements.sent-2411-851: The first strategy, known as textual inversion, utilises a pretrained text-to-music model to map audio input to corresponding ``pseudowords" in the textual embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-852: The second strategy involves training a music language model from scratch, jointly with a text conditioner and a quantised audio feature extractor.
elements.sent-2411-853: This approach allows for the combination of textual and audio conditioning during inference, balanced through a novel double classifier-free guidance method.
elements.sent-2411-854: The paper contributes to the field by adapting the textual inversion method to a pretrained text-to-music model and introducing a style conditioner method based on a frozen audio feature extractor, a transformer encoder, a Residual Vector Quantizer (RVQ), and temporal downsampling.
elements.sent-2411-855: The model's components include a style conditioner that is jointly trained with the language model.
elements.sent-2411-856: During training, a 30-second music excerpt paired with a textual description is input to the model.
elements.sent-2411-857: The textual description is processed through a frozen T5 tokenizer and transformer encoder, while the style encoder takes a random subsample of the input audio and encodes it.
elements.sent-2411-858: The text and style latent representations are projected to match the dimension of the transformer language model and provided as a prefix to the sequence to model.
elements.sent-2411-859: The input audio is encoded by a pretrained EnCodec model, and the language model is trained autoregressively with a cross-entropy loss.
elements.sent-2411-860: Additionally, the tokens corresponding to the random subsample fed into the style encoder are masked in the loss to reduce the model's tendency to copy the style audio input.
elements.sent-2411-861: The textual inversion method involves optimising the textual embedding by taking successive gradient steps on the cross-entropy loss of the music language model.
elements.sent-2411-862: The style conditioning method uses a style conditioner with bottlenecks (RVQ and downsampling) to prevent transmitting all the information of the conditioning audio excerpt to the model.
elements.sent-2411-863: The double classifier-free guidance formula is given by: \begin{equation}l_{\text{double CFG}} = l_{\emptyset} + \alpha \left[ l_{\text{style}} + \beta (l_{\text{text},\text{style}} - l_{\text{style}}) - l_{\emptyset} \right],\end{equation} where \( l_{\text{style \) and \( l_{\text{text ,\text{style \) are the logits of the model conditioned on style and textual description, respectively, and \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \) are parameters used to balance the importance of the style and text conditioning.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-864: These interfaces aim to assist users in the music creation process by providing tools that are both powerful and intuitive.
elements.sent-2411-865: Some of these interfaces are built upon AI models~\citep{cococo, visualisation, iteratta , while others extend traditional music software with AI capabilities, such as Band-in-a-Box.\footnote{\url{https://www.pgmusic.com/ CoCoCo~\citep{cococo is an interactive interface based on the CoCoNet model~\citep{coconet , trained on Bach's chorales to assist users in composing four-part harmonies.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-866: The system allows users to steer the AI generation process, enabling a collaborative composition experience.
elements.sent-2411-867: These AI-based interfaces offer varying degrees of control over the music creation process; however, their functionality is typically tied to a single backend model, which limits their adaptability and the range of tasks they can support.
elements.sent-2411-868: While these models represent a significant advancement, their interfaces are often limited to simple text prompts and lack the interactive refinement capabilities that musicians may require.
elements.sent-2411-869: However, there are a few key developments in this area.
elements.sent-2411-870: One promising direction is the development of multiple control features, which can be enhanced through graphical user interfaces (GUIs).
elements.sent-2411-871: With the appearance of libraries like Gradio~\footnote{\url{https://www.gradio.app/ , it has become easier to create standard interfaces that facilitate model interaction.}}
elements.sent-2411-872: Recent text-to-music models such as Suno~\footnote{\url{https://suno.com/ and Udio~\footnote{\url{https://www.udio.com/ have introduced user-friendly interfaces for tasks like music inpainting, music continuation, and lyric modification.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-873: These platforms aim to provide users with greater control over their music generation processes and incorporate a range of AIGC functionalities.
elements.sent-2411-874: For example, the interfaces of Suno (Figure~\ref{fig:suno}\footnote{Credit from \url{https://www.reddit.com/r/SunoAI/comments/1e1tumo/customized_create_screen_for_suno/ ) and Udio (Figure~\ref{fig:udio ) adopt the layouts similar to traditional music or audio editing software, often divided into three sections: the left panel for navigation or track management, the central panel for interaction with control modules (such as adjusting musical parameters or selecting generation options), and the right panel displaying a list of generated music outputs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-875: This design, which closely resembles the structure of popular audio editing tools, appears to be becoming the standard in the field.
elements.sent-2411-876: On the product level, it is evident that many developers are converging on this layout, likely because it offers a familiar and intuitive user experience, allowing users to easily manage and refine their generated content.
elements.sent-2411-877: While such integrations are not yet mainstream, they represent a significant step toward combining the flexibility of traditional music production with the power of AI.
elements.sent-2411-878: However, there remains a gap in the literature regarding the effectiveness of these interfaces, particularly in terms of user experience and usability.
elements.sent-2411-879: Recent studies have also explored the use of text-to-audio models in interactive interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-880: These works highlight the potential and challenges of interactive music creation interfaces powered by AI.
elements.sent-2411-881: While significant progress has been made, there remains a need for interfaces that are both flexible and intuitive, supporting a wide range of creative tasks and allowing for natural interaction between the user and the system.
elements.sent-2411-882: Text-to-music generation, as a form of interactive interface, opens new possibilities for next-generation music creation tools, although it is not the only path forward.
elements.sent-2411-883: Our work seeks to demonstrate the potential of such interfaces while acknowledging the diversity of approaches in this evolving field.
elements.sent-2411-884: Datasets play a crucial role in training and evaluating text-to-music generation models and interactive music creation interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-885: However, the availability of large-scale, publicly accessible datasets for music generation is limited due to licensing restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-886: Many models are trained on proprietary datasets comprising vast amounts of music data that are not publicly released.
elements.sent-2411-887: For evaluation purposes, smaller datasets with text-audio pairs have been developed.
elements.sent-2411-888: Each example in MusicCaps includes a 10-second music clip from the AudioSet dataset \citep{audioset and a detailed caption focusing on how the music sounds, rather than metadata like artist names.}
elements.sent-2411-889: Another dataset is the {Song Describer Dataset (SDD) \citep{mulab , which contains approximately 1.1k captions for 706 music recordings.}}
elements.sent-2411-890: SDD is designed for evaluating models that address music-and-language tasks such as music captioning, text-to-music generation, and music-language retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-891: The dataset provides comprehensive descriptions that can be used to assess the quality of generated music in terms of adherence to textual prompts.
elements.sent-2411-892: Additionally, several other datasets have the potential to contribute to the training of text-to-music datasets.
elements.sent-2411-893: For text-based music editing and controllable music generation, standard datasets are scarce.
elements.sent-2411-894: Researchers often generate their own datasets by processing existing music data.
elements.sent-2411-895: In the context of controllable music generation, datasets with metadata or extracted audio features are commonly used to provide control signals.
elements.sent-2411-896: For example, models may use tempo, pitch, or instrumentation information extracted from music data to condition the generation process.
elements.sent-2411-897: However, there is no standardised dataset for controllable music generation, and researchers often rely on custom datasets tailored to their specific needs.
elements.sent-2411-898: For instance, \citet{instructme created the {InstructME dataset by collecting 417 hours of music and generating triplet data (instruction, source music, target music) for various editing tasks such as remixing, adding, removing, extracting, and replacing musical elements.}}
elements.sent-2411-899: Similarly, \citet{audit generated triplet training data for their model {AUDIT , focusing on editing tasks like adding, dropping, replacement, inpainting, and super-resolution.}}
elements.sent-2411-900: The workflow for triplet data generation in the InstructME dataset is as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-901: Remix: Randomly select a clip and mix all its instrument tracks to create the source music.
elements.sent-2411-902: Obtain rhythm and timing information from the source music, then retrieve another clip with the same rhythm to serve as the target music.
elements.sent-2411-903: Align the source and target music using time-step information and generate the instruction using a template like ``Remix with \{instrument/genre\'' .}
elements.sent-2411-904: Add: Choose a clip and select \( i \) instrument tracks (where \( i \in [1, 2, 3, 4] \)) to mix into the source music.
elements.sent-2411-905: Create the target music by adding another instrument track to the source music.
elements.sent-2411-906: The instruction follows a template such as ``Add \{instrument\'' .}
elements.sent-2411-907: Remove: Reverse the adding operation by using the target music from the adding task as the source and the source music as the target.
elements.sent-2411-909: Extract: Select one instrument track from a clip to serve as the target music.
elements.sent-2411-910: Mix this track with additional instruments to create the source music.
elements.sent-2411-912: Replace: Choose two different instrument tracks and mix them with other instruments to form the source and target music.
elements.sent-2411-913: The instruction template is ``Replace \{instrument A\ with \{instrument B\}'' .}
elements.sent-2411-914: They provided specific text command templates and examples for each task to facilitate the model's learning of diverse editing instructions.
elements.sent-2411-915: Examples include ``Add distorted electric guitar,'' ``Remove accordion,'' ``Extract viola,'' ``Replace flute with accordion,'' and ``Remix with drums, bass, guitar, piano.'' This structured approach to dataset construction enabled the model to learn various editing operations in a controlled and scalable manner.
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{tabular}{ll} Task Text Command Template Remix Remix with \{instrument/genre\} Add Add \{instrument\} Remove Remove \{instrument\} Extract Extract \{instrument\} Replace Replace \{instrument A\} with \{instrument B\} \end{tabular} \caption{Text command templates used for generating instructions in the InstructME dataset~\cite{instructme}.}
elements.sent-2411-916: Evaluating the performance of text-to-music generation models involves both \highlight{objective and subjective metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-917: Subjective metrics, on the other hand, are designed to evaluate human perceptions of attributes such as musicality, creativity, and adherence to input prompts.
elements.sent-2411-918: Metrics Several quantitative metrics have been proposed to evaluate different aspects of generated music:.
elements.sent-2411-919: Fréchet Distance (FD): The Fréchet Distance measures the similarity between the statistical distributions of the generated audio and a reference dataset.
elements.sent-2411-920: Traditionally, FD is computed using features extracted by models like VGGish \citep{fad , which operates at 16~kHz.}
