elements.sent-2411-921: However, for high-fidelity audio at higher sampling rates, FD\textsubscript{OpenL3 has been introduced.}
elements.sent-2411-922: OpenL3 \citep{openl3 accepts audio signals up to 48~kHz, allowing for evaluation of full-bandwidth audio.}
elements.sent-2411-923: FD\textsubscript{OpenL3 computes the Fréchet Distance in the OpenL3 feature space, capturing perceptual aspects of audio quality and diversity in generated samples.}
elements.sent-2411-924: Kullback-Leibler Divergence (KL): The KL divergence measures the difference between two probability distributions.
elements.sent-2411-925: In this context, KL\textsubscript{PaSST uses PaSST \citep{passt , a state-of-the-art audio tagging model trained on AudioSet \citep{audioset , to compute the KL divergence over the predicted class probabilities between the generated and reference audio.}}}
elements.sent-2411-926: A lower KL\textsubscript{PaSST indicates that the generated audio shares similar semantic content with the reference audio, reflecting the model's ability to generate relevant content.}
elements.sent-2411-927: CLAP Score: The CLAP (Contrastive Language-Audio Pretraining) score \citep{clap computes the cosine similarity between the embeddings of the input text prompt and the generated audio, as obtained from a CLAP model.}
elements.sent-2411-928: A higher CLAP score indicates better alignment between the textual description and the generated audio, measuring how well the model captures the semantics of the input prompt.
elements.sent-2411-929: These metrics have been adapted to handle variable-length and high-resolution audio.
elements.sent-2411-930: For instance, to evaluate long-form audio, KL\textsubscript{PaSST can be computed by segmenting the audio into overlapping windows and averaging the predictions \citep{musicgen .}}
elements.sent-2411-931: Similarly, the CLAP score can be calculated using a feature fusion approach, combining embeddings from different segments of the audio to handle durations longer than the model's default input length \citep{musicgen .}
elements.sent-2411-932: These adaptations ensure that the metrics accurately reflect the quality and relevance of longer and higher-fidelity audio samples generated by modern models.
elements.sent-2411-933: Subjective evaluation remains crucial in assessing the quality of generated music, as it captures human perceptions that are difficult to quantify objectively.
elements.sent-2411-934: Human listeners are typically asked to rate samples based on criteria such as:.
elements.sent-2411-935: Overall Quality (OVL): Listeners rate the perceptual quality of the audio, considering factors like sound fidelity, absence of artifacts, and general pleasantness.
elements.sent-2411-936: Relevance to Text Input (REL): Listeners assess how well the generated music matches the given text prompt, evaluating the semantic alignment between the description and the audio.
elements.sent-2411-937: Studies like those by \citet{musicgen and \citet{noise2music have conducted human evaluations using platforms such as Amazon Mechanical Turk.}}
elements.sent-2411-938: These studies ensure the reliability of the evaluations by filtering out outliers and ensuring that listeners fully engage with the samples.
elements.sent-2411-939: For instance, \citet{musicgen use the CrowdMOS package to filter noisy annotations and remove unreliable raters.}
elements.sent-2411-940: Subjective evaluations provide insights into the musicality and creative aspects of the generated music that are not captured by quantitative metrics.
elements.sent-2411-941: For music editing tasks, additional metrics are essential for evaluating the effectiveness of editing operations.
elements.sent-2411-942: Metrics such as the Scale-Invariant Signal-to-Distortion Ratio (SI-SDR) \citep{si-sdr are commonly used to assess the quality of source separation or the removal of specific tracks from a mix.}
elements.sent-2411-943: SI-SDR evaluates the fidelity of the reconstructed signal compared to the original source while disregarding scale differences.
elements.sent-2411-944: Furthermore, classifiers can be used to verify whether certain attributes have been successfully modified, such as confirming whether an instrument has been replaced or a genre has been changed.
elements.sent-2411-945: Pretrained classifiers or evaluators provide a quantitative measure of whether the desired editing operations have been performed as expected.
elements.sent-2411-946: Several additional metrics can also be employed to address specific aspects of music editing.
elements.sent-2411-947: For instance, in InstructME, the instruction accuracy (IA) metric measures the relevance of the text-music pair by calculating the accuracy of edited music tags, such as instrument, mood, and genre, compared to the input command.
elements.sent-2411-948: Similarly, in Music ControlNet, various metrics are used to evaluate the editing process.
elements.sent-2411-949: Melody accuracy checks whether the pitch classes (C, C\#, etc.) align between the input melody control and the generated output.
elements.sent-2411-950: Dynamics correlation, calculated using Pearson’s correlation, assesses the relationship between the input and generated dynamics values, both on a micro and macro scale.
elements.sent-2411-951: Micro correlation focuses on individual generations, while macro correlation evaluates across multiple generations.
elements.sent-2411-952: Finally, Rhythm F1, based on beat/downbeat detection, quantifies the alignment of timestamps between the input rhythm control and the generated output, considering alignment within 70 milliseconds.
elements.sent-2411-953: In conclusion, this chapter has provided a comprehensive overview of the key concepts, models, and techniques that form the foundation of text-to-music generation and editing systems.
elements.sent-2411-954: I have explored various representations of music and text, examined the architecture and functionality of backbone models such as diffusion models and audio language models, and discussed the importance of controllability and editability in music generation.
elements.sent-2411-955: The background presented here sets the stage for the novel contributions of this thesis, which directly address some of the most pressing challenges in the field of AI-driven music creation and manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-956: Our work builds upon and extends the concepts discussed in this chapter in several key ways.
elements.sent-2411-957: MusicMagus: Inference-Time Optimisation Model for Music Editing.
elements.sent-2411-958: This contribution leverages the understanding of backbone models and control mechanisms to develop a novel approach that allows for precise editing of generated music at inference time.
elements.sent-2411-959: This work aims to provide a more flexible and responsive tool for the music editing task.
elements.sent-2411-960: Instruct-MusicGen: Instruct tuning for music editing.
elements.sent-2411-961: Building on the Parameter-Efficient Finetuning (PEFT) techniques discussed, such as LoRA and Llama-Adapter, our instruct tuning approach demonstrates how pretrained models can be efficiently adapted for specific music editing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-962: This work showcases the practical application of the theoretical concepts of model adaptation and specialised editing models outlined in this chapter.
elements.sent-2411-963: Loop Copilot: Agent-based music editing model.
elements.sent-2411-964: This work combines the power of large language models with multiple music generation models.
elements.sent-2411-965: It builds upon the agent-based methods for compositional music generation and editing discussed in this chapter, extending these concepts to create a more interactive and intuitive music editing experience.
elements.sent-2411-966: These contributions collectively advance the state-of-the-art in controllable and editable music generation, which are two key challenges identified in our overview of current models and techniques.
elements.sent-2411-967: By grounding our work in the fundamental concepts and recent advancements outlined in this chapter, we position our research at the forefront of AI-driven music creation and manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-968: The following chapters will delve deeper into the methodologies, implementations, and results of these novel approaches, demonstrating how they push the boundaries of what is possible in AI-assisted music composition and editing.
elements.chapter-2411-005: Loop Copilot: Conducting AI Ensembles for Music Generation and Iterative Editing
elements.sent-2411-969: This chapter represents the initial exploration into the challenge of text-based music editing within the context of AI-assisted music creation.
elements.sent-2411-970: The work presented here builds on two contributions: COSMIC, an early-stage proof of concept, and Loop Copilot, an extended system that addresses some of the limitations observed in COSMIC.
elements.sent-2411-971: COSMIC proposed the innovative idea of using a combination of multiple specialised music models, coordinated by a state machine, to facilitate music composition through a simple text-based interface.
elements.sent-2411-972: However, the system's reliance on a manually designed state machine and its limited interaction capabilities highlighted the need for a more adaptable and user-friendly approach.
elements.sent-2411-973: Loop Copilot extends this concept by integrating a large language model (LLM) as an AI agent, thereby improving the system’s usability and making the interaction more natural for users.
elements.sent-2411-974: This chapter also includes a user study to evaluate the effectiveness and limitations of Loop Copilot in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-975: The core contribution of this chapter lies in the introduction and detailed examination of Loop Copilot.
elements.sent-2411-976: The system is designed to enable users to generate and iteratively refine music through a multi-round dialogue interface, powered by an LLM that interprets user intentions and selects appropriate AI models for specific tasks.
elements.sent-2411-977: These models, each specialised in different aspects of music creation, work together to achieve the user's goals, with their outputs being aggregated to maintain coherence in the musical piece.
elements.sent-2411-978: A key innovation in Loop Copilot is the Global Attribute Table (GAT), a dynamic data structure that records and preserves essential musical attributes throughout the iterative process.
elements.sent-2411-979: This ensures that the integrity of the music is maintained as modifications are made.
elements.sent-2411-980: The chapter concludes with a user study, conducted through semi-structured interviews and questionnaires, which provides valuable insights into the system’s practical utility, as well as its strengths and areas for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-981: Positioned as the foundational work in this thesis, this chapter sets the stage for the subsequent exploration of more advanced text-guided music editing techniques.
elements.sent-2411-982: While Loop Copilot demonstrates the feasibility and potential of a multi-model approach to music creation, it also exposes limitations, particularly in its ability to perform precise and detailed edits.
elements.sent-2411-983: These limitations serve as a catalyst for the development of the more sophisticated methods discussed in the following chapters, particularly MusicMagus and Instruct-MusicGen, which aim to address these challenges and further advance the state of the art in AI-assisted music production.\footnote{Code available at \url{https://github.com/ldzhangyx/loop-copilot .}}
elements.sent-2411-984: Music creation is an art that has traditionally been the domain of expert human musicians.
elements.sent-2411-985: Recently, with the advent of artificial intelligence (AI) music models~\citep{survey , the music creation process is becoming more democratised.}
elements.sent-2411-986: However, in the real world, there are two major challenges in the human music creation process: first, music creation involves multiple phased tasks, from harmony and melody crafting, to arrangement and mixing; second, music creation is an inherently iterative process that cannot be achieved in one step.
elements.sent-2411-987: It usually undergoes multiple refinements before reaching its final form.
elements.sent-2411-988: Most current AI models, including interactive music interfaces and dedicated generative models, fall short in at least one of these two challenges.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/preview.pdf} \caption{A conceptual illustration of interaction with Loop Copilot. The diagram depicts a two-round conversation: initially, a user requests music generation and the AI provides a loop. In the subsequent round, the user seeks modifications, and the AI offers a refined loop, emphasising Loop Copilot's iterative feedback-driven music creation process.} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-989: Interactive music interfaces excel in melody inpainting ~\citep{cococo, visualisation but often lack adaptability for diverse music creation.}
elements.sent-2411-990: Current popular interactive music interfaces, such as Magenta Studio~\citep{magentastudio and Flow Machines~\footnote{\url{https://www.flow-machines.com/ , are powerful and user-friendly, but they predominantly focus on a singular type of musical modification: melody inpainting—filling in gaps based on an existing melody.}}}
elements.sent-2411-991: These models, with their intuitive human-in-the-loop interactions, have undoubtedly lowered the entry barrier for users.
elements.sent-2411-992: However, these AI-based interfaces for music creation, although recognising the importance of iterative generation and refinement, often rely on a single task throughout the process.
elements.sent-2411-993: This reliance not only hampers their flexibility but also restricts their adaptability to diverse music creation needs.
elements.sent-2411-994: On the other hand, dedicated music models offer broad capabilities but tend to have a narrow focus, limiting their application.
elements.sent-2411-995: Existing dedicated music generative models have demonstrated significant capabilities across a myriad of tasks in music creation, such as controlled music generation using chord progressions~\citep{polyffusion, inpainting , text prompts~\citep{musicgen,musiclm , images \citep{vis2mus , and emotion~\citep{fadernets .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-996: They also span a spectrum of music style transfer tasks at the score~\citep{polydis,accomontage , performance~\citep{mididdsp , and timbre~\citep{timbretransfer levels.}}}
elements.sent-2411-997: However, a prevalent issue with these models is their `one-off' design approach.
elements.sent-2411-998: They often treat music generation as a singular process, either focusing strictly on music generation or specific editing tasks, like style transfer.
elements.sent-2411-999: As a result, users looking to engage in a comprehensive music creation process find themselves scouting for various models to cater to different aspects of their musical needs.
elements.sent-2411-1000: This chapter introduces Loop Copilot, a system designed to address these challenges.
elements.sent-2411-1001: It allows users to generate a music loop and iteratively refine it through a multi-round dialogue with the system.
elements.sent-2411-1002: By leveraging a large language model~\citep{llmsurvey , Loop Copilot seamlessly integrates various specialised models catering to different phases of music creation.}
elements.sent-2411-1003: It harnesses the power of individual models to provide a rich set of generation and editing tools.
elements.sent-2411-1004: The intuitive and unified interaction is facilitated through a conversational interface, reminiscent of the benefits of the first type of above models, while applying the strengths of the second type.
elements.sent-2411-1005: Loop Copilot is built on three key components: a large language model (LLM) controller, which interprets user intentions, selects suitable AI models for task execution, and gathers the outputs of these models; a set of backend AI models, which carry out specific tasks; and a Global Attribute Table (GAT), which records necessary music attribute information to ensure continuity throughout the creation process.
elements.sent-2411-1006: Intuitively, users can utilise the LLM to `conduct' the AI ensemble, guiding the music creation process through conversation.
elements.sent-2411-1007: In summary, the main contributions are:.
elements.sent-2411-1008: This chapter introduces Loop Copilot, a novel system that integrates LLMs with specialised AI music models.
elements.sent-2411-1009: This enables a conversational interface for collaborative human-AI creation of music loops.
elements.sent-2411-1010: This chapter develops the Global Attribute Table that serves as a dynamic state recorder for the music loop under construction, thereby ensuring that the musical attributes remain consistent in the iterative editing process.
elements.sent-2411-1011: This chapter conducts an interview-based comprehensive evaluation, which not only measures the performance of our system but also sheds light on the advantages and limitations of using an LLM-driven iterative editing interface in music co-creation.
elements.sent-2411-1012: The COSMIC system serves as the foundation for Loop Copilot, sharing the underlying concept of using a form-filling approach to maintain consistency in music creation.
elements.sent-2411-1013: In COSMIC, the design focuses on two main aspects: building a dialogue system and enabling controlled music generation.
elements.sent-2411-1014: The dialogue system is adaptable to the needs of music composition through the use of a dialogue tracker, which creates a form instance for each music creation task.
elements.sent-2411-1015: This form records the status of the music creation and translates the user's inputs into form-filling actions.
elements.sent-2411-1016: Once the form is completed, COSMIC passes the information to the backend models responsible for music and lyric generation.
elements.sent-2411-1017: In the current version, COSMIC employs the BUTTER model~\citep{butter for controlled music generation, which utilises natural language as a condition, along with a GPT-2 based model for controlled lyric generation.}
elements.sent-2411-1018: Figure \ref{fig:steps shows the working mechanism of COSMIC during a single-round dialogue with an example.}
elements.sent-2411-1019: The user says to COSMIC, "I want to create a slow and sad piece of music".
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{figs/2.png} \caption{A diagram of COSMIC's handling of a single round of dialogue.} \label{fig:steps}
elements.sent-2411-1020: This sentence is first transferred from COSMIC's front-end to the NLP interface in RESTful format and received by the Natural Language Understanding (NLU) module, as shown in step 1.
elements.sent-2411-1021: The NLU module parses the user's requirements and keywords from text input and transfers them to the Dialogue State Tracker (DST) module in Dialogue Manager in json format, as shown in step 2.
elements.sent-2411-1022: After receiving the data, the DST module fills in the creation form and determines the current dialogue status.
elements.sent-2411-1023: After determining the status, the DST transmits the creation form and the current status to the POL module as shown in step 3.
elements.sent-2411-1024: The Dialogue Policy (POL) module is responsible for converting the creation table into a condition and sending conditional generation commands to the model layer as shown in step 4.
elements.sent-2411-1025: After POL gets the generated music data from the model layer, it sends a response command to the NLG module as shown in step 5.
elements.sent-2411-1026: The Natural Language Generation (NLG) module translates the command into natural language that humans can understand and sends it back to the COSMIC front-end along with the music data as shown in step 6;.
elements.sent-2411-1027: Finally, COSMIC logs this session to the database as shown in step 7.
elements.sent-2411-1028: I begin with an example of a typical interaction process, shown in Figure \ref{fig:teaser .}
elements.sent-2411-1029: It comprises two key steps: the user initially:.
elements.sent-2411-1030: After completing the 2-round dialogue, the current status can be represented by a sequence $[(Q_1, A_1), (Q_2, A_2)]$, where $Q$ means the user's question and $A$ means the answer of Loop Copilot.
elements.sent-2411-1031: To formally define the interaction process, let us consider a sequence $H_T = [(Q_1, A_1), ..., (Q_T, A_T)]$, where each $(Q_t, A_t)$ pair denotes a user query and the corresponding system response in the $t$-th round dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-1032: At each step $t$, the system generates a response $A_t$ using the Loop Copilot function: \begin{equation}A_t = LoopCopilot(Q_t, H_{t-1}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1033: Figure \ref{fig:diagram shows the workflow of our proposed system.}
elements.sent-2411-1034: Loop Copilot comprises 5 key components: (1) The large language model ($\mathcal{M$) for understanding and reasoning; (2) The system principles ($\mathcal{P$) that provide basic rules to guide the large language model; (3) A list of backend models ($\mathcal{F$) responsible for executing specific tasks; (4) A global attribute table ($\mathcal{T$) that maintains crucial information to ensure continuity throughout the creative process; and (5) A framework handler ($\mathcal{D$) that orchestrates the interactions between these components.}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/diagram_v2_2.pdf} \caption{Loop Copilot's workflow. Once the user inputs the request, firstly, Loop Copilot preprocesses the input and converts it to textual modality; secondly, the LLM, based on the input, the system principles, and the chat history, performs the task analysis and calls the corresponding models; after that, the backend models execute the task and output the result; finally, the LLM does the final processing of the output and returns it.} \label{fig:diagram}
elements.sent-2411-1035: The workflow of Loop Copilot involves several steps.
elements.sent-2411-1036: The framework handler $\mathcal{D}$ utilises a music captioning model to describe the input music, while textual inputs are kept as they are.
elements.sent-2411-1037: It calls the large language model $\mathcal{M}$ to analyse the task, resulting in a sequence of steps, which may involve a call to a single model or multiple chained calls to models, as the large language model may need to handle the task step by step.
elements.sent-2411-1039: After task analysis, the framework handler records all the steps and proceeds to execute the tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1040: It calls the backend models in the specified order, providing them with the necessary parameters obtained from the large language model.
elements.sent-2411-1041: If it requires a chained call of multiple models, the intermediate results generated by the previous model will be used in the next model.
elements.sent-2411-1042: Once the task execution is complete, the handler $\mathcal{D}$ collects the final result and sends it to the large language model for the final output.
elements.sent-2411-1043: Throughout this process, all operations are tracked and recorded in the global attribute table $\mathcal{T}$, ensuring consistency and continuity in the generation process.
elements.sent-2411-1044: Algorithm \ref{alg:workflow illustrates the process during a T-round dialogue.}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{The workflow of Loop Copilot}\label{alg:workflow} \renewcommand{\algorithmicrequire}{Input:} \renewcommand{\algorithmicensure}{Output:} \begin{algorithmic} \Require user queries $Q = \{Q_1, ..., Q_T\}$ \Ensure responses $A = \{A_1, ..., A_T\}$ \State Initialise components: $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{F}, \mathcal{T}, \mathcal{D}$ \State Initialise chat history $H_0$ \State Define $A_0$ as initial music state or silence \highlight{\State At timestep $T$:} \State $Q'_t \gets \mathcal{D}(Q_t)$ \Comment{Input preprocessing} \State $\mathcal{F}_{1:N} \gets \mathcal{M}(Q'_t, H_{t-1})$ \Comment{Task analysis} \State $A'_{t,0} \gets A_{t-1}$ \Comment{initialise the chain} \For{$n$ in $[1, N]$} \State $A'_{t, n} \gets \mathcal{F}_n(A'_{t,n-1})$ \Comment{Task execution} \EndFor \State $A_t \gets \mathcal{M}(A'_{t,N})$ \Comment{Response generation} \State $H_t \gets$ Append$(H_{t-1}, (Q_t, A_t))$ \Comment{Update chat history} \State Update $\mathcal{T}$ with key attributes from $A_t$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-1045: The interaction process within Loop Copilot is essentially a multi-stage workflow, as illustrated in Figures \ref{fig:teaser and \ref{fig:diagram .}}
elements.sent-2411-1046: The first stage involves the user drafting a music loop, while the second stage is dedicated to iterative refinement through dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-1048: In the initial stage, the focus is on creating music from an ambiguous demand, essentially with a global description.
elements.sent-2411-1049: The later stages shift the focus to music editing, where fine-grained localised revisions are made.
elements.sent-2411-1050: These revisions can include regenerating specific areas, adding or removing particular instruments, and incorporating sound effects.
elements.sent-2411-1051: A comprehensive list of all supported tasks is presented in Table \ref{tab:tasks .}

elements.sent-2411-1072: The Global Attribute Table (GAT) is an integral component of the Loop Copilot system, designed to encapsulate and manage the dynamic state of music being generated and refined during the interaction process.
elements.sent-2411-1073: Its role is to offer a centralised repository for the various attributes that define the musical piece at any given moment.
elements.sent-2411-1074: This centralisation is pivotal for Loop Copilot's ability to provide continuity, facilitate task execution, and maintain musical coherence.
elements.sent-2411-1075: The design philosophy behind the GAT draws inspiration from ``blackboard" architectures~\citep{blackboard .}
elements.sent-2411-1076: In this paradigm, the GAT can be likened to a blackboard---a shared workspace where different components of the system can access and contribute information.

elements.sent-2411-1078: GAT's significance can be further expounded upon through its multifaceted functionalities:.
elements.sent-2411-1079: State Continuity: The GAT ensures that users experience a seamless dialogue with the Loop Copilot by persistently tracking musical attributes and evolving based on both user input and system output.
elements.sent-2411-1080: Task Execution: During the task execution phase, backend models often require contextual information.
elements.sent-2411-1081: The GAT provides this context, thereby enhancing the models' performance.
elements.sent-2411-1082: Musical Coherence: For any music creation tool, maintaining musical coherence is paramount.
elements.sent-2411-1083: By storing key attributes like musical key and tempo, the GAT ensures the harmonious and consistent evolution of music throughout the creative process.
elements.sent-2411-1084: In practice, the GAT information is attached to the system prompt, served as an additional document to be retrieved.
elements.sent-2411-1085: It is important to clarify that the implementation of GAT imposes no vocabulary constraints; it is populated with relevant keywords in natural language form.
elements.sent-2411-1086: To evaluate the efficacy and usability of Loop Copilot, a mixed-methods experimental design was adopted.
elements.sent-2411-1087: This design aligns with the triangular research framework~\citep{triangle .}

elements.sent-2411-1089: I recruited 8 volunteers (N=8) who were interested in AI-based music production, and work in the field of music and audio technology or production, though not necessarily professional-level musicians.
elements.sent-2411-1090: Participants provided informed consent, and data anonymisation protocols were strictly followed to maintain ethical standards ~\footnote{The experiment was approved by Yamaha ethics committee.. .}
elements.sent-2411-1091: The distribution of the participants was as follows:.

elements.sent-2411-1096: Usability serves as a critical metric for assessing the ease with which users can interact with Loop Copilot.
elements.sent-2411-1097: It measures not only the system's efficiency but also gauges the intuitive nature of the user interface.
elements.sent-2411-1098: I adopted the System Usability Scale (SUS)~\citep{sus (5-point Likert scale, see Appendix A) as a validated tool for this aspect of the evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-1099: After linear projection, SUS scores have a range from 0 to 100, where a value over 68 is considered acceptable.
elements.sent-2411-1100: Understanding user acceptance is crucial for assessing whether Loop Copilot would be willingly incorporated into existing workflows.
elements.sent-2411-1101: This encompasses factors like the perceived ease of use and the perceived usefulness of the system.
elements.sent-2411-1102: The Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)~\citep{tam served as the theoretical framework for evaluating these dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-1103: Our TAM questionnaire (5-point Likert scale, see Appendix B) consists of 11 questions categorised into perceived ease of use (Q1-4), perceived usefulness (Q5-8), and overall impressions (Q9-11).
elements.sent-2411-1104: Beyond usability and acceptance, the qualitative aspect of user experience provides a more nuanced understanding of the system's impact.
elements.sent-2411-1105: This involves exploring the emotional and cognitive perceptions that users have when using Loop Copilot, such as the joys and frustrations they experience.
elements.sent-2411-1106: Open-ended questions were designed to capture these subjective aspects in detail.
elements.sent-2411-1107: Experiments were conducted in a quiet, controlled environment to ensure consistency and minimize distractions.
elements.sent-2411-1108: The experimental session for each participant consisted of three phases:.
elements.sent-2411-1109: Orientation Phase (10 minutes): During this phase, participants were acquainted with the functionalities and features of Loop Copilot.
elements.sent-2411-1110: This briefing aimed to standardize the initial level of understanding across participants.
elements.sent-2411-1111: Specifically, the system was shown to the subjects with a brief explanation of how to use the interface.
elements.sent-2411-1112: Furthermore, the participants were presented with the example inputs in Table~\ref{tab:tasks as examples of possible prompts supported by the system.}
elements.sent-2411-1113: Interactive Usage Phase (20 minutes): Participants were allowed to freely interact with Loop Copilot for music composition.
elements.sent-2411-1114: Observational notes were made in real-time to capture immediate insights and identify areas for potential system improvement.
elements.sent-2411-1115: Feedback and Evaluation Phase (15 minutes): Upon completion of the interaction, participants were asked to fill out the SUS and TAM questionnaires.
elements.sent-2411-1116: Additionally, a semi-structured interview based on the responses from the questionnaires was conducted to obtain qualitative feedback on their experience.
elements.sent-2411-1117: Both quantitative (SUS, TAM scores) and qualitative (interview notes) data were collected.

elements.sent-2411-1148: Given the small sample size (N=8), we conducted a manual analysis based on the interview notes.
elements.sent-2411-1149: For each participant, we carefully reviewed the feedback provided and identified recurring themes mentioned by at least two individuals.
elements.sent-2411-1150: This process allowed us to categorise the feedback into four main areas, as outlined below.
elements.sent-2411-1151: The analysis aimed to capture key patterns in user experience, which were then triangulated with the quantitative findings to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the system’s performance and user reception.
elements.sent-2411-1152: Participants generally found value in Loop Copilot as a tool for music generation.
elements.sent-2411-1153: Users widely considered it a promising starting point for creative inspiration.
elements.sent-2411-1154: Some participants found text-to-music conversion not fully meeting their specific musical visions, indicating a gap between user expectations and system output.
elements.sent-2411-1155: Participants thought that Loop Copilot was useful for getting creative inspiration.
elements.sent-2411-1157: Most participants, especially beginners and intermediates, appreciated the intuitive nature of the interface.
elements.sent-2411-1158: Most users found the system to be straightforward and easy to understand.
elements.sent-2411-1159: Design and Interaction Users lauded the design potential and interactive methods, suggesting that they represent a fertile ground for future development.
elements.sent-2411-1161: Participants commonly mentioned the limited control they had over the musical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-1162: Some cited specific instances where text prompts like ``Add a rhythmic guitar" or ``Remove reverb" were not adequately reflected in the output.
elements.sent-2411-1164: Some users thought the system's current specifications were limited as a stand-alone music production system, and wanted it instead as a part of existing music creation systems, like a digital audio workstation.
elements.sent-2411-1165: Many users called for additional features like volume control, the ability to upload their own melody lines, and options for chord conditioning.
elements.sent-2411-1166: Users also highlighted the need for multiple output options to choose from, rather than a single output.
elements.sent-2411-1167: Given that some participants found the system occasionally unresponsive to specific prompts, they hoped future versions could offer improved interpretation and execution of user commands.
elements.sent-2411-1168: This chapter presented Loop Copilot, a system designed to integrate Large Language Models (LLMs) with specialised AI music models to support human-AI collaborative creation of music loops.
elements.sent-2411-1169: The system utilises a conversational interface that allows users to engage in an interactive and iterative music creation process.
elements.sent-2411-1170: A key innovation of Loop Copilot is the introduction of the Global Attribute Table, which tracks the evolving state of the music to ensure that modifications are coherent and consistent throughout the editing process.
elements.sent-2411-1171: Additionally, the system employs a chaining mechanism that enables training-free music editing by leveraging existing AI music models.
elements.sent-2411-1172: Our evaluation, supported by interview-based insights, demonstrates the potential of conversational interfaces for facilitating iterative music production.
elements.sent-2411-1173: While the current system demonstrates the feasibility of controlling non-text-to-music generation models, such as VampNet, there are limitations in the precision and scope of music editing tasks that can be achieved.
elements.sent-2411-1174: The input and output remain constrained by the capabilities of the individual models.
elements.sent-2411-1175: Although chaining multiple models offers some degree of control, it is not sufficient for more complex tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1176: For instance, there is currently no model that can precisely add a new track to existing music audio based on a natural language description of the track—a limitation that has inspired the development of Instruct-MusicGen (Chapter~\ref{ch:instruct ).}
elements.sent-2411-1177: Similarly, there are other potential text-based controls that require deeper exploration into text-to-music foundation models, motivating the research behind MusicMagus (Chapter~\ref{ch:musicmagus ).}
elements.sent-2411-1178: In summary, while Loop Copilot provides a usable interface for AI-assisted music creation, its limitations of the performance of specific components highlight the need for more advanced models and techniques.
elements.sent-2411-1179: These insights have directly informed the subsequent research presented in this thesis, where I seek to overcome these challenges and further advance the capabilities of text-based music production.
elements.chapter-2411-006: MusicMagus: Zero-Shot Text-to-Music Editing via Diffusion Models
elements.sent-2411-1180: This chapter introduces MusicMagus, a system developed to explore the challenges associated with text-guided music editing.
elements.sent-2411-1181: Building on insights gained from Loop Copilot, MusicMagus presents a novel approach that leverages pre-trained diffusion models to enable zero-shot music editing.
elements.sent-2411-1182: The system is designed to modify specific musical attributes, such as genre, mood, and instrumentation, while preserving the overall structural integrity of the original piece.
elements.sent-2411-1183: This capability is particularly significant given the iterative nature of music production, where nuanced and flexible edits are often required to achieve the desired artistic outcome.
elements.sent-2411-1184: MusicMagus operates by manipulating the latent space of diffusion models, transforming the text-based editing process into a task of latent space exploration.
elements.sent-2411-1185: This method incorporates an additional constraint that enforces consistency, ensuring that the edits do not disrupt non-targeted musical elements.
elements.sent-2411-1186: By adopting this approach, MusicMagus demonstrates that stylistically coherent edits can be achieved without additional training, positioning it as a versatile tool for real-world music editing scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-1188: The system primarily focuses on intra-stem editing, i.e. making changes within individual musical stems, but encountering challenges with more complex operations, such as adding or removing instruments.
elements.sent-2411-1189: Additionally, the diffusion-based approach is inherently constrained by the length of the generated audio, which is typically shorter than what can be produced by music language models.
elements.sent-2411-1190: These limitations highlight opportunities for further advancements in text-guided music editing, motivating the development of the more sophisticated system, Instruct-MusicGen, discussed in the following chapter.
elements.sent-2411-1191: Recent advances in text-to-music generation have opened up new possibilities in musical creativity~\citep{butter,accomontage,musecoco,polyffusion,musiclm,mousai,musicgen,musicldm .}
elements.sent-2411-1192: However, a significant challenge persists in how to edit the generated results as music production usually involves iterative refinements.
elements.sent-2411-1193: Building on this momentum, I regard `text-to-music editing' as the process of using text queries to edit music, and I see two major types of operations: inter-stem editing, such as adding or removing instruments (e.g., ``add a saxophone" or ``remove the drums"), and intra-stem editing, which involves modifications within the same stem, such as adding effects or changing instruments (e.g., ``add reverb to this stem" or ``transfer the timbre of the specified notes").
elements.sent-2411-1194: In this context, a ``stem'' refers to an individual track or component within a music piece, such as a specific instrument or vocal part.
elements.sent-2411-1195: The primary focus of this paper is on the latter, intra-stem editing.
elements.sent-2411-1196: One of the fundamental challenges of text-to-music editing is the difficulty of accommodating flexible text operations in both dataset construction and model training.
elements.sent-2411-1197: This is not only a matter of data pair scarcity, but also of the complexity inherent in the vast array of possible text-based edits that can be applied to music.
elements.sent-2411-1198: Existing research~\citep{audit,instructme,m2ugen has primarily focused on manually constructing datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-1199: However, these models are constrained to a few predefined operations, which undermines their effectiveness in text-to-music editing, which requires flexibility and variety.
elements.sent-2411-1200: This highlights the need for a new approach that moves away from traditional supervised learning reliant on specific data pairs and toward a more adaptable, unsupervised, or zero-shot approach.
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/task.pdf} \caption{Text-to-music editing with MusicMagus. The edit from ``piano" to ``acoustic guitar" in the text prompt directly alters the corresponding musical attribute, while leaving others unchanged.} \label{fig:musicmagus-teaser}
elements.sent-2411-1201: In this work, I introduce MusicMagus, which focuses on text-based intra-stem music editing.
elements.sent-2411-1202: Leveraging the inherent capabilities of pre-trained diffusion models, MusicMagus is able to perform zero-shot editing without requiring additional training pairs.
elements.sent-2411-1203: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:musicmagus-teaser , I utilise word swapping to direct the editing process.}
elements.sent-2411-1204: This operation is implemented as a manipulation within the diffusion model's semantic space.
elements.sent-2411-1205: Recognising the sensitivity of the diffusion process, where minor alterations can propagate significant changes, I employ an additional constraint to ensure that the resultant music maintains the structural integrity and stylistic coherence of the original music.
elements.sent-2411-1206: Although I mainly focus on the editing of music clips generated from diffusion models, I also discuss how to edit real-world music audio using the Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM) inversion technique ~\citep{ddim .}
elements.sent-2411-1207: In summary, the main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-1208: I propose a flexible and user-friendly text-to-music editing method using word swapping.
elements.sent-2411-1209: I contribute MusicMagus, a system capable of zero-shot music editing on diverse tasks without any dependence on paired training data.
elements.sent-2411-1210: Comparative experiments validate that MusicMagus outperforms existing zero-shot methods and some supervised approaches in tasks such as style and timbre transformation.
elements.sent-2411-1211: To illustrate the idea, I refer to the example in Figure~\ref{fig:musicmagus-teaser .}

elements.sent-2411-1213: The next step involves altering this text prompt by substituting ``piano" with ``acoustic guitar", thereby creating a new prompt $y'$.

elements.sent-2411-1374: In this chapter, I explored MusicMagus, a system developed to investigate the challenges and possibilities of text-guided music editing through latent space manipulation within pre-trained diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-1375: MusicMagus represents a significant step in AI-assisted music creation, offering a method to modify specific musical attributes while preserving the structural integrity of the original music.
elements.sent-2411-1376: This approach, which does not require additional training, demonstrates the potential for stylistically coherent edits in real-world music production scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-1377: However, MusicMagus also has its limitations.
elements.sent-2411-1378: The system’s focus on latent space manipulation constrains its ability to perform more complex editing tasks, such as adding or removing entire musical elements.
elements.sent-2411-1379: Additionally, the diffusion models used are limited by the length of the generated audio, making them less suitable for longer music sequences compared to music language models.
elements.sent-2411-1380: These limitations suggest areas for further research and innovation in text-guided music editing, leading to the development of Instruct-MusicGen.
elements.sent-2411-1381: By examining these challenges, Instruct-MusicGen aims to expand the scope of music editing operations, offering greater flexibility and precision in handling more complex and dynamic music production scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-1382: In general, MusicMagus contributes to the evolving landscape of AI-assisted music creation, highlighting both the potential and the challenges of zero-shot text-to-music editing.
elements.sent-2411-1383: It sets the stage for future explorations that aim to push the boundaries of what is possible in this emerging field.
elements.chapter-2411-007: Instruct-MusicGen: Unlocking Text-to-Music Editing for Music Language Models via Instruction Tuning
elements.sent-2411-1384: This chapter introduces Instruct-MusicGen, a system designed to address the limitations of existing text-to-music editing models by enhancing both the precision and flexibility of music generation.
elements.sent-2411-1385: Building on the foundational work of Coco-mulla and the advancements realised in MusicMagus, Instruct-MusicGen offers a novel approach that integrates instruction tuning with a dual-modality fusion process.
elements.sent-2411-1386: This integration allows the model to interpret complex text-based editing instructions and apply them directly to musical content, thereby enabling more nuanced and dynamic modifications.
elements.sent-2411-1387: Instruct-MusicGen operates by concurrently processing textual instructions and audio inputs, a capability made possible through the introduction of text and audio fusion modules.
elements.sent-2411-1388: These modules allow for precise control over the editing process, whether it involves adding, removing, or altering specific musical stems.
elements.sent-2411-1389: This system is particularly significant in the context of music production, where the ability to make detailed, text-guided edits, improves AI models' usability for achieving specific artistic goals.
elements.sent-2411-1390: While Instruct-MusicGen builds on the strengths of earlier systems like MusicMagus by broadening the scope of text-to-music editing, it also addresses some of the limitations observed in previous approaches.
elements.sent-2411-1391: Specifically, it overcomes challenges related to intra-stem editing and the constraints of diffusion models by leveraging the capabilities of music language models.
elements.sent-2411-1392: However, Instruct-MusicGen is not without its own challenges, such as issues related to signal-level precision and the reliance on paired data for fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-1393: These aspects highlight the areas where further research is needed to fully realise the potential of text-to-music editing.
elements.sent-2411-1394: By carefully examining both its capabilities and limitations, this chapter aims to position Instruct-MusicGen within the broader context of this thesis, contributing to the ongoing exploration of text-guided music generation.
elements.sent-2411-1395: Through this analysis, I will explore how Instruct-MusicGen advances the field while also acknowledging the opportunities for future work that remain.
elements.sent-2411-1396: As discussed in Chapter~\ref{ch:background , there is a growing interest in the development of models that offer greater controllability~\citep{cocomulla, musiccontrolnet, mustango, airgen and editability~\citep{instructme, musicmagus, m2ugen over the music generation process.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1397: In music production, a stem, a mixed group of tracks often related by instrument type (like drums or lead vocals), is essential for mixing and mastering because it allows producers to isolate, adjust, and manipulate individual elements of a song.
elements.sent-2411-1398: Following the definition in Chapter~\ref{ch:musicmagus , ``text-to-music editing" involves using textual queries to modify various aspects of a music recording, which can be categorised into two main types: intra-stem editing, which focuses on modifying a single stem (e.g., changing the instrument, timbre, or performance style) and inter-stem editing, which involves altering the relationships among stems (e.g., adding, removing, or separating stems).}
elements.sent-2411-1399: The work mainly focuses on the problem of inter-stem editing.
elements.sent-2411-1400: Previous attempts to develop text-based music editing models have encountered several challenges.
elements.sent-2411-1401: Some approaches~\citep{audit,instructme have focused on the training of specialised editing models from scratch, which is resource-intensive and may not yield results comparable to state-of-the-art music generation models.}
elements.sent-2411-1402: Other work~\citep{loopcopilot, m2ugen, uniaudio has sought to leverage existing language models (LLMs) and MusicGen~\citep{musicgen , allowing the LLM to interpret editing instructions without further training the music model.}}
elements.sent-2411-1403: Although this approach offers flexibility, it often lacks the ability to precisely reconstruct the conditional audio, leading to unreliable results.
elements.sent-2411-1404: To address these limitations, an ideal solution should harness the knowledge embedded in pretrained models to ensure high-quality audio output while adapting the architecture to accommodate the specific requirements of music editing tasks.

elements.sent-2411-1408: To train Instruct-MusicGen, I synthesise an instructional dataset using the Slakh2100 dataset~\citep{slakh , introducing only ~8\% additional parameters compared to the original model, and finetune the model for only 5K steps, which is less than 1\% of training a music editing model from scratch.}
elements.sent-2411-1409: I evaluate Instruct-MusicGen on two datasets: the Slakh test set and the out-of-domain MoisesDB dataset~\citep{moisesdb .}

elements.sent-2411-1412: The main contributions of this work are threefold:.
elements.sent-2411-1413: I propose Instruct-MusicGen, a novel approach that finetunes pretrained large music language models to follow editing instructions effectively.
elements.sent-2411-1414: This method addresses the limitations of previous approaches by leveraging pretrained models to ensure high-quality audio output and precise reconstruction, combining both music and text multi-modal control.
elements.sent-2411-1415: I enhance the capabilities of a pretrained text-to-music model to perform multiple tasks, including adding, separating, and extracting stems from music audio, all within a single training process.
elements.sent-2411-1416: This approach significantly reduces the computing resources cost compared to training specialised editing models from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-1417: I extend the benchmarking for text-to-music editing tasks, providing a comprehensive comparison across various models.
elements.sent-2411-1418: The model outperforms existing baselines and achieves performance comparable to models specifically trained for individual tasks, demonstrating the effectiveness and versatility of the approach.
elements.sent-2411-1419: Before introducing Instruct-MusicGen, it is important to first understand its precursor, Coco-mulla.
elements.sent-2411-1420: I contributed to this work as a co-author, and through expanding its concepts within the field of music editing, we developed Instruct-MusicGen.
elements.sent-2411-1421: However, Coco-mulla is not presented as a primary contribution in my thesis.
elements.sent-2411-1422: Coco-mulla is a framework developed to explore the integration of content-based controls within music generation, specifically addressing the limitations of models that rely solely on text-based descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-1423: Previous text-based controls, while functional, often fall short in capturing intricate musical elements such as chord progressions, rhythm patterns, and melodic structures.
elements.sent-2411-1424: Coco-mulla seeks to address these challenges by introducing a joint embedding approach that combines both symbolic (e.g., chord and MIDI representations) and acoustic (e.g., drum patterns) features within a unified generative model.
elements.sent-2411-1425: Coco-mulla is implemented using a parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) framework designed to enhance pre-trained music language models, such as MusicGen, without necessitating extensive retraining.
elements.sent-2411-1426: The model introduces two primary components: a joint symbolic and acoustic embedding encoder and a condition adaptor.
elements.sent-2411-1427: The joint embedding encoder in Coco-mulla is designed to integrate symbolic music data and acoustic signals into a coherent representation.
elements.sent-2411-1428: Symbolic music data is encoded using MIDI and chord structures.
elements.sent-2411-1429: For instance, a chord is represented as a combination of its root pitch and bass pitches, as well as chroma, forming a multi-hot vector $\mathbf{c _i$ for each frame $i$: \begin{equation}c_i =  [e(root); e(bass); m; 0], if chord exists in frame  i [0;0;0; 1], otherwise \end{equation} where $e(\text{root )$ and $e(\text{bass )$ are basis vectors representing the root and bass pitches, and $\mathbf{m}$ is the chroma vector.}}}
elements.formula-2411-067: \begin{equation}c_i =  [e(root); e(bass); m; 0], if chord exists in frame  i [0;0;0; 1], otherwise \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1430: Acoustic features, particularly drum tracks, are encoded using pre-trained embeddings derived from EnCodec tokens.
elements.sent-2411-1431: These embeddings are then mapped to a lower-dimensional space via a trainable matrix, facilitating the model's ability to manage continuous representations effectively.
elements.sent-2411-1432: The condition adaptor is critical for integrating the joint embeddings into the generative process.
elements.sent-2411-1433: This module modifies the self-attention layers of the pre-trained MusicGen model by introducing a condition prefix that captures the temporal evolution of symbolic and acoustic conditions.
elements.sent-2411-1434: The condition prefix is inserted into the final layers of the MusicGen decoder, where it interacts with the hidden states derived from the original text-based prompts.
elements.sent-2411-1435: The condition information is processed through a series of self-attention and cross-attention mechanisms, ensuring that the generated music adheres to both the symbolic and acoustic conditions provided.
elements.sent-2411-1436: This foundation, while robust in its approach, highlighted several areas where further advancements could be made, particularly in enhancing the flexibility and precision of music editing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1437: These insights directly informed the development of Instruct-MusicGen, which aimed to build upon and extend the capabilities introduced by Coco-mulla.
elements.sent-2411-1438: Building upon the framework established by Coco-mulla, Instruct-MusicGen was developed to address some of the limitations and expand the potential of content-based controls in music generation.
elements.sent-2411-1439: By incorporating a more sophisticated instruction-following mechanism, Instruct-MusicGen enables dynamic and precise text-based editing of music, thus offering greater flexibility and control over the generated output.
elements.sent-2411-1440: While Coco-mulla utilises a joint embedding strategy to merge symbolic and acoustic controls, Instruct-MusicGen introduces an additional text fusion module.
elements.sent-2411-1441: This module enables the model to interpret and execute complex editing instructions alongside processing audio inputs.
elements.sent-2411-1442: The architecture of Instruct-MusicGen integrates the LLaMA-adapter framework into the condition adaptor, enhancing its capacity to manage both symbolic and text-based controls.
elements.sent-2411-1443: Moreover, Instruct-MusicGen employs a dual-modality fusion process, where the audio fusion module combines the original music input with the desired edits specified by text instructions.
elements.sent-2411-1444: This process allows Instruct-MusicGen to perform tasks such as adding or removing specific instruments, changing musical styles, or separating stems with a higher degree of precision, which Coco-mulla’s original implementation was not designed to achieve to the same extent.
elements.sent-2411-1445: The primary distinction between Coco-mulla and Instruct-MusicGen is the latter's ability to handle complex editing tasks using text instructions without compromising on the quality or fidelity of the generated music.
elements.sent-2411-1446: While Coco-mulla provides a framework for generating music based on combined symbolic and acoustic conditions, it does not offer the same level of flexibility required for detailed music editing—an area where Instruct-MusicGen shows marked improvements.
elements.sent-2411-1447: Instruct-MusicGen also benefits from an optimised training process.
elements.sent-2411-1448: Despite introducing only $\sim$8\% additional parameters compared to the original MusicGen model, it achieves enhanced performance across a range of editing tasks as seen in Table~\ref{tab:editing_4_stems .}
elements.sent-2411-1449: The use of instruction tuning, combined with the advanced fusion modules, allows Instruct-MusicGen to adapt and edit existing music more effectively than its predecessor.
elements.sent-2411-1450: The following sections detail the architecture, training strategy, and inference methods implemented in Instruct-MusicGen, highlighting how these innovations build upon the groundwork laid by Coco-mulla.
elements.sent-2411-1451: Instruct-MusicGen takes a music audio input $X^{\text{cond }$ and a text instruction $X^{\text{instruct }$ (e.g., ``Add guitar") as inputs.}}
elements.sent-2411-1452: The model then edits the music audio $X^{\text{cond }$ according to the instruction $X^{\text{instruct }$ and generates the desired edited music $X^{\text{music }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1453: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:2 , Instruct-MusicGen incorporates two additional modules into the vanilla MusicGen: an audio fusion module and a text fusion module.}
elements.sent-2411-1454: Note that we no longer use the symbolic conditioning in Coco-mulla, which is because audio conditioning has already contained all information of the input stems.

elements.sent-2411-1455: The audio fusion module enables Instruct-MusicGen to accept external audio inputs, which is inspired by \citet{llamaadapter and \citet{cocomulla .}}
elements.sent-2411-1456: Figure~\ref{fig:2 presents the diagram of the audio fusion module.}

elements.sent-2411-1458: Similarly, I transform $X^{\text{music }$ into the pretrained embedding $z^{\text{music }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1459: The module begins by duplicating self-attention modules of the pretrained MusicGen model to extract latent representations of $z^{\text{cond }$.}
elements.sent-2411-1460: Given that MusicGen consists of $M$ layers, I denote \begin{equation}Z^{\text{cond}} = \{z^{\text{cond}}_0, z^{\text{cond}}_1, \ldots, z^{\text{cond}}_M\},\end{equation} \begin{equation}Z^{\text{music}} = \{z^{\text{music}}_0, z^{\text{music}}_1, \ldots, z^{\text{music}}_M\},\end{equation} which represent the hidden states of $X^{\text{cond }$ and $X^{\text{music }$ respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-1461: Note that I use a learnable input embedding as $z^{\text{cond _0$ and initialise $z^{\text{music _0$ with $z^{\text{music }$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1462: I compute the vanilla self attention for $X^{\text{music }$ as follows: \begin{equation}Q^{\text{music}}_l, K^{\text{music}}_l, V^{\text{music}}_l = \text{QKV-projector}(z^{\text{music}}_l),\end{equation} \begin{equation}o^{\text{music}}_l = \text{SelfAttn}(Q^{\text{music}}_l, K^{\text{music}}_l, V^{\text{music}}_l).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1463: I project $z^{\text{cond }$ to a high-dimension representation $h$ via a linear layer $f_l$ and learnable positional encoding $e_l$, \begin{equation}h=f_l(z^{\text{cond}}) + e_l.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1464: Then, I compute the $(l+1)$-th layer hidden states of $X^{\text{cond }$ as follows: \begin{equation}Q^{\text{cond}}_l, K^{\text{cond}}_l, V^{\text{cond}}_l = \text{QKV-projector}(z^{\text{cond}}_l + h),\end{equation} \begin{equation}z^{\text{cond}}_{l+1} = {\rm SelfAttn}(Q^{\text{cond}}_l, K^{\text{cond}}_l, V^{\text{cond}}_l).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1465: To fuse information of $X^{\text{cond }$ into $X^{\text{music }$, I compute the cross attention between them, \begin{equation}s^{\text{music}}_l = \text{CrossAttn}(Q^{\text{music}}_l+Q^{\text{cond}}_l, K^{\text{cond}}_l, V^{\text{cond}}_l).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-1466: Finally, the attention output of $X^{\text{music }$ is updated as follows, \begin{equation}s'_{l} = o^{\text{music}}_l + g_l \cdot s^{\text{music}}_l,\end{equation} \begin{equation}z^{\text{music}}_{l+1} = \text{TextFusion}(s'_{l}, X^{\text{instruct}}), \label{eq:textfusion}\end{equation} where $g$ is a zero-initialised learnable gating factor.}
elements.sent-2411-1467: Thus, the total trainable parameters in Instruct-MusicGen include the input embedding $z^{\rm cond _0$, linear layers $f_l$, learnable position embeddings $e_l$, learnable gating factors $g$, and learnable parameters in the text fusion module.}

elements.sent-2411-1469: I achieve this by finetuning only the cross-attention module between the text embedding and the music representations while keeping the text encoder's parameters frozen.

elements.sent-2411-1474: I conduct both subjective experiments and objective experiments for evaluation, and also provide audio samples in Figure~\ref{fig:subfigures and a demo page~\footnote{\url{https://bit.ly/instruct-musicgen .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1475: For the objective evaluations, I utilise two distinct datasets, each serving a specific purpose in assessing both in-domain and out-of-domain performance capabilities of various models.
elements.sent-2411-1477: The Synthesised Lakh (Slakh) Dataset, originally derived from the Lakh MIDI Dataset v0.1, comprises audio tracks synthesised using sample-based virtual instruments.
elements.sent-2411-1478: Known as Slakh2100, this dataset features 2100 tracks complete with corresponding MIDI files.
elements.sent-2411-1479: These tracks are methodically divided into subsets designated for training (1500 tracks), validation (375 tracks), and testing (225 tracks), collectively amounting to approximately 145 hours of audio mixtures.
elements.sent-2411-1480: This dataset primarily supports audio source separation tasks within a controlled, synthesised environment.
elements.sent-2411-1482: In contrast to Slakh2100, the MoisesDB dataset caters to source separation with a broader scope.
elements.sent-2411-1483: It includes 240 real audio tracks sourced from 45 diverse artists spanning twelve musical genres.
elements.sent-2411-1484: Uniquely, MoisesDB organises its tracks into a detailed two-level hierarchical taxonomy of stems, offering a varied number of stems per audio track, each annotated with textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-1485: The rationale for selecting two datasets lies in their diverse configurations and common applications.
elements.sent-2411-1486: While the Slakh dataset is typically utilised for training models tailored to a four-stem arrangement \citep[e.g.,][]{GMSDI , the model, Instruct-MusicGen, although initially trained on this dataset, is designed to generalise to various stem configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-1487: Conversely, models such as InstructME and AUDIT are trained on private or larger, more diverse datasets.
elements.sent-2411-1488: By employing both Slakh2100 and MoisesDB, I ensure a comprehensive evaluation, allowing us to fairly compare the adaptability and performance of different models under varying conditions of data familiarity and complexity.
elements.sent-2411-1489: I utilised the Slakh2100 dataset to construct an instruction-based dataset for training and testing, employing the following pipeline:.
elements.sent-2411-1490: A data point was randomly selected from the Slakh training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-1491: The target stem represents the difference between the input and output, where the input consists of $n$ stems, and the output consists of $1$, $n-1$ or $n+1$ stems \footnote{The number of both input stems and output stems should range from $[1, N]$, where $N$ is the total number of audio stems .}
elements.sent-2411-1492: For example, if the chosen instruction is``add guitar", the target stem would be the distinction between the input and output, with the input containing $n$ stems and the output containing $n+1$ stems.
elements.sent-2411-1493: Then, $n$ other stems were randomly chosen from the remaining stems to complete the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-1494: An offset was randomly determined to cut a 5-second audio clip.
elements.sent-2411-1495: If the target stem contained more than 50\% silence, a different offset was selected.
elements.sent-2411-1496: The stems were mixed according to the specified instructions to create a triplet consisting of \{instruction text, condition audio input, audio ground truth\}.
elements.sent-2411-1497: For the finetuning of MusicGen, I jointly trained the audio fusion module and the text fusion module.
elements.sent-2411-1498: The optimisation process utilised the AdamW optimiser, with a learning rate set at $5e^{-3}$.
elements.sent-2411-1499: I use L2 loss over latent token embeddings as the training objective.
elements.sent-2411-1500: Training incorporated a Cosine Annealing scheduler with an initial warmup of 100 steps.
elements.sent-2411-1501: The training regimen extended over 5,000 steps with an accumulated batch size of 32, achieved through setting the batch size to 8 and using gradient accumulation over 4 iterations.
elements.sent-2411-1502: The finetuning process was executed on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU and was completed within a span of two days.
elements.sent-2411-1503: In this section, I explore two baseline models, each distinguished by their unique methodologies for handling audio data.
elements.sent-2411-1504: AUDIT~\citep{audit (Chapter \ref{sec:audit ): AUDIT is an instruction-guided audio editing model, consisting of a variational autoencoder (VAE) for converting input audio into a latent space representation, a T5 text encoder for processing edit instructions, and a diffusion network that performs the actual audio editing in the latent space.}}
elements.sent-2411-1505: The system accepts mel-spectrograms of input audio and edit instructions, and generates the edited audio as output.
elements.sent-2411-1506: M$^2$UGen~\citep{m2ugen (Chapter \ref{sec:m2ugen ): The M$^2$UGen framework leverages large language models to comprehend and generate music across various modalities, integrating abilities from external models such as MusicGen~\citep{musicgen and AudioLDM 2~\citep{audioldm2 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1507: InstructME (Chapter \ref{sec:instructme ) is a novel framework for instruction-guided music editing and remixing that leverages latent diffusion models.}
elements.sent-2411-1508: However, InstructME's model weights and evaluation protocol have note been publicly released, so it is only possible to record the evaluation results which were reported in the original paper.

elements.sent-2411-1510: Having been introduced in Chapter~\ref{ch:background , the metrics to evaluate model performance are listed below.}
elements.sent-2411-1512: I use the FAD score to evaluate the overall audio quality of the predicted music.
elements.sent-2411-1514: A high CLAP score indicates the predicted music aligns with the text description well.
elements.sent-2411-1516: A low KL score indicates the predicted music shares similar features with the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-1517: Structural Similarity (SSIM)~\citep{ssim is an image quality metric that I adapt to evaluate structural similarity between predicted music and ground truth.}
elements.sent-2411-1518: Scale-Invariant Signal-to-Distortion Ratio (SI-SDR) \citep{si-sdr quantifies audio quality, especially in source separation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-1519: It is scale-invariant, useful for varying audio volumes, and measures distortion relative to a reference signal.
elements.sent-2411-1520: I use SI-SDR score to evaluate the signal loss of the predicted audio.
elements.sent-2411-1521: Scale-Invariant Signal-to-Distortion Ratio improvement (SI-SDRi) \citep{si-sdri extends SI-SDR, measuring the improvement in signal-to-distortion ratio after processing.}
elements.sent-2411-1522: It is commonly used in audio enhancement and separation contexts.
elements.sent-2411-1523: To further investigate if the model can successfully add, remove or extract the instrument, I propose the P-Demucs score to evaluate model performance.
elements.sent-2411-1524: This metric specifically focuses on detecting the presence of a newly added instrument in the generated audio.
elements.sent-2411-1525: It leverages the Demucs model, a source separation model, to isolate the target instrument from the audio.
elements.sent-2411-1526: After separation, the root-mean-square energy (RMSE) of the isolated track is used for judging~\footnote{\url{https://librosa.org/doc/main/generated/librosa.feature.rms.html .}}
elements.sent-2411-1527: For example, if the instruction is to ``add guitar," the success of the model is indicated by the presence of a non-silent guitar track.
elements.sent-2411-1528: P-Demucs (Precision over Demucs) measures the precision of this detection, focusing exclusively on the new instrument's presence without considering changes to existing instruments.
elements.sent-2411-1529: The evaluation of Instruct-MusicGen demonstrates its superior performance across various tasks compared to existing text-to-music editing baselines (AUDIT, InstructME, M$^2$UGen).
elements.sent-2411-1530: On the Slakh dataset (Table~\ref{tab:editing_4_stems ), Instruct-MusicGen performs well in adding, removing, and extracting stems, achieving the lowest Fréchet Audio Distance (FAD) and the highest CLAP and SSIM scores in the addition task.}
elements.sent-2411-1531: It also significantly improved the signal-to-noise ratio (SI-SDR) in the removal task, showing balanced performance across all metrics and proving its robustness in various editing scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-1532: It achieved competitive FAD scores and showed improvements in CLAP and SSIM metrics for both addition and removal tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1533: Our model consistently outperformed baseline models, highlighting its efficiency and effectiveness in text-to-music editing applications.

elements.sent-2411-1544: Instruction Adherence (IA) assesses how accurately the generated music follows the given editing instruction.
elements.sent-2411-1545: In this experiment, participants rate the generated music on a scale from 1 to 5, where 1 indicates that the instruction was not followed at all, and 5 indicates that the instruction was followed perfectly.
elements.sent-2411-1546: For example, if the instruction is ``Remove Drums," a rating of 1 would mean that the drums were not removed at all, while a rating of 5 would mean that the drums were completely removed.
elements.sent-2411-1547: Audio Quality (AQ) evaluates the overall audio quality of the generated music in comparison to the original music.
elements.sent-2411-1548: Participants rate the audio quality on a scale from 1 to 5, where 1 represents very poor quality with significant degradation compared to the original music, and 5 represents excellent quality, as good as or better than the original music.
elements.sent-2411-1549: This metric helps in understanding how the editing process affects the overall sound quality of the music.

elements.sent-2411-1555: The total trainable parameters include the input embedding layer, linear layers for joint embedding learning, and gate scalars, and the linear layers make up over 80\% of the total parameters.
elements.sent-2411-1556: In this ablation, I want to investigate whether the parameters can be reduced by revising the architecture of the linear layers.
elements.sent-2411-1557: The original linear layers have the shape of $(48, 2048, 2048)$, where 48 is the number of layers of the of MusicGen model, and $(2048, 2048)$ is a linear transformation from embedding to layer-wise representation.
elements.sent-2411-1558: I replace a single layer with an auto-encoder architecture, consisting of two linear layers: $(2048, M)$ and $(M, 2048)$, where $M \ll 2048$.
elements.sent-2411-1559: I record and compare the loss on the validation set between the variant and the original Instruct-MusicGen model, shown in Figure~\ref{fig:ablation_L .}
elements.sent-2411-1560: The variation has $M=12$, which reduces the parameter size from 201M to 23M.
